questions.questionText:
In a calendar year, how many minimum sessions
must take place for the Parliament to fulfil its
constitutional responsibility of assembly?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:**
● India does not have a fixed parliamentary calendar. The **only constitutional requirement is that** **there should** **not be more than six months between two sessions.**
● According to **Article 85 of the Constitution of India,** the President shall from time to time summon each House of Parliament to meet at such time and place as he thinks fit, but **six months shall not** **intervene** between its last sitting in one session and the date appointed for its first sitting in the next session. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to Lok Sabha Ethics Committee:
I. - Its members are appointed by the Speaker for
a period of one year.
II. 	 It can take punitive action against the member
accused of breach of ethics.
III. 	 It can take up only cases of misconduct that
involve MPs.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:  **Lok Sabha Ethics Committee** is tasked with overseeing the moral and ethical conduct of the members and examining cases referred to it with reference to ethical and other misconduct of members.  The ethics committee was constituted in 2000, to oversee the moral and ethical conduct of members and examine cases of ‘unethical conduct’ referred to it. The members of the Ethics Committee are **appointed by the Speaker for a period of one year**. **So, statement I is correct.**  In case of rule violations by House officers or employees, **the committee can recommend actions** **such as dismissal of the MP, reprimand, fine**, or any other appropriate punishment. The **committee only has a recommendatory role** and cannot take any punitive action. **So, statement** **II is not correct.**  The Committee examines complaints filed against members of the House by other members; outsiders through a member; or referred by the Speaker. The Committee makes a prima facie inquiry before deciding to examine a complaint and presents its report to the Speaker, who places it before the House for consideration. **The term ‘unethical’ is not defined.** It is left to the Committee to decide whether any act is unethical or not. The Ethics Committee can take up only cases of misconduct that involve MPs. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, only two of the above statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct
with regard to the Departmentally Related
Standing Committees (DRSCs)?
I. - The members of DRSCs are elected by the
members of the House amongst themselves.
II. 	 All DRSCs have members both from the Lok
Sabha and the Rajya Sabha.
III. 	 DRSCs cannot examine the demands for
grants of ministries and departments.
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Departmentally Related Standing Committees (DRSCs)**, are constituted to **assist** **Parliament in scrutinising funds allocated to Ministries.** They also examine Bills referred to them by Parliament and analyse other relevant policy issues.
● At present, there are 24 DRSCs that oversee the working of a Ministry or group of Ministries. **Each of** **them are composed of 31 members: 21 from Lok Sabha and 10 from Rajya Sabha.** These DRSCs are constituted for a period of one year. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Members of the DRSCs **are nominated by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha** and the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, respectively, **not directly elected** by the entire House. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Key functions of the Departmentally Related Standing Committees (DRSCs):** o **Examine the Demands for Grants** of various ministries and departments and submit a report
to the House.  **So, statement III is not correct.** o **Examining bills referred to them** by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha or the Chairman of the
Rajya Sabha, provided they pertain to their concerned ministries or departments. o **Consider annual reports of ministries and departments** and review long-term national
policy documents, again if referred to them by the presiding officers of the respective Houses. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **DRSCs submit reports to** **Parliament after examining Bills, Demands for** **Grants** and issues and submit Action Taken Reports which show the recommendations accepted by the government, and the progress made on them.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The Governor of a State has a constitutionally
prescribed term of five years.
II. 	 No grounds are mentioned in the Constitution
for the removal of the Governor.
III. 	 In case of vacancy, the Chief Justice of the
High Court functions as the Governor of the
State.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation  :**
● The Governor of a State is the executive head of the State appointed by the President at his/her pleasure. **Article 156** of the Constitution of India outlines the terms of office for the Governor.
● As per the article, **a term of five years has been prescribed for the Governor-in-office**, but the same is **subjected to the Doctrine of Pleasure of the President**, essentially meaning the President can remove or transfer the Governor before this prescribed term or can even extend the term. Also, being an **office of continuity** the incumbent Governor needs to hold office until his/her successor takes charge. Although **Article 156 prescribes a five-year term,** it is subject to the pleasure of the President. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Constitution of India **does not mention any specific grounds** for the **removal of a Governor**. **So, statement II is correct.**
o Also the Governor can resign at any time by addressing a resignation letter to the President.
● Unlike the Office of the President, for which there is a prescribed chain of command to be invoked in case of vacancy (Vice President, Chief Justice of India, Senior-most judge of the Supreme Court and so on) there is not any similar provision for the office of the Governor. **Article 160 empowers the** **President to make provisions for the Governor's functions when unforeseen circumstances** **arise** that are not specifically covered in the Constitution. Hence, **it is up to the President to decide** **who will take charge of the office in case of a vacancy**. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Governor of a State can reserve a bill for the consideration of the President even without ministerial advice.
**Statement II:** Providing assent to bills is a constitutionally recognised discretionary power of the Governor under Article 200. **Statement III:** The decision of the Governor to reserve bills for the President is not subject to Judicial Review.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct, but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement–I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under **Article 200 of the Constitution of India**, when a Bill passed by the State Legislature is presented, the Governor **may assent to the Bill, withhold assent, reserve it for the President’s** **consideration, or return it (except a Money Bill) for reconsideration.**
● The **Governor is generally bound by the advice of the Council of Ministers**, as per Article 163, and must act accordingly under Article 200 except in exceptional and narrowly **defined circumstances** **where the Governor can use the discretion**. Such discretion is valid only in rare and serious situations, for instance:
o If the Bill clearly violates the Constitution (e.g., conflicts with Fundamental Rights or falls within
the Union List);
o If it threatens the unity, integrity, or sovereignty of the country;
o If it encroaches on the legislative domain of Parliament.
● In these cases, the Governor may reserve the Bill for the President even without ministerial advice. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● However, the power to grant or withhold assent is **not an unfettered discretionary power.** The Governor is expected to act on the advice of the Council of Ministers, and cannot arbitrarily withhold assent to delay or block legislation. As clarified by the **Supreme Court in** **State of Tamil Nadu v** **Governor of Tamil Nadu,** **2025** withholding assent is not a constitutionally recognised discretionary power, except in exceptional cases of constitutional concern. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● In the same case the Court further held that the **decision to reserve a Bill for the President is** **subject to judicial review.** The Court held that although Article 200 allows some discretionary action, such discretion is not absolute, and must be exercised within constitutional bounds and subject to judicial scrutiny. Further stating that exempting such decisions from judicial review would violate the core principle that all constitutional authorities are accountable under the Constitution. **So, statement III** **is not correct.** **So, neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - A person must be qualified for election as a member of the Lok Sabha to be eligible for the office of the President and the VicePresident of India.
II. 	 The President of India may tender his/her resignation to the Vice-President and vice versa.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Union executive consists of the President, the Vice-President, and the Council of Ministers** **with the Prime Minister** as the head to aid and advise the President. Executive power of the Union is vested in the President, and is exercised by him either directly or through officers subordinate to him in accordance with the Constitution. The Vice-President is ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha and acts as President when the latter is unable to discharge his functions due to absence, illness or any other cause, or till the election of a new President.
● Article 58 provides that no person shall be eligible for election as President unless – he/she is a citizen of India, has completed the age of thirty-five years, and **is qualified for election as a member of the** **House of the People (Lok Sabha)**.
● Article 66 says that no person shall be eligible for election as Vice-President (VP) unless - he is a citizen of India; has completed the age of thirty-five years; and is **qualified for election as a member of the** **Council of States (Rajya Sabha).**
○ While the first two eligibility conditions are same for both the offices, for the **President a** **person needs to be eligible for being a member of Lok Sabha, while for VP’s office** **one must be eligible for the membership of Rajya Sabha**. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● According to Article 56, the **President may address his/her resignation to the Vice-President.** Under Article 67, **the Vice- President may address his resignation to the President**. **So,** **statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The President is elected by the members of an electoral college consisting of **the** **elected members of both the Houses of Parliament** and the elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of States and the Union Territories of Delhi and Pondicherry.
● The **Vice-President is elected by the members of an electoral college** **consisting of the members of both Houses of Parliament** (both elected and nominated)**.** He is elected in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote through a secret ballot.
○ Thus, while in the **Presidential election only elected members** of both the Houses participate, in the **Vice-Presidential election both elected and** **nominated members** of the Houses participate.
**Relevance:** Recently, C.P. Radhakrishnan was elected as the 17th Vice-President of India.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Parliamentary Device Feature
I. - Calling Attention Motion Not subject to the vote of the House
II. - Adjournment Motion
Has an element of censure against the government
III. - Censure Motion If passed, the
Minister must resign
IV. - Zero Hour Informal device for members to raise important matters
V. - Special Mention
Usually raised by an opposition member in order to control the government
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The term **`motion**' in parliamentary parlance means any formal proposal made to the House by a Member for the purpose of eliciting a decision of the House.
● **Calling Attention:** A Member may, with the prior permission of the Speaker, **call the attention of** **a Minister to any matter of urgent public importance** and the Minister may make a brief statement thereon.
○ There shall be no debate on such a statement at the time it is made. After the statement, brief clarifications can be sought from the Minister by the Member who has initiated the Calling Attention and other Members whose names appear in the List of Business are called by the Speaker. Only those matters which are primarily the concern of the Union Government can be raised through a Calling Attention notice. ○ The Calling Attention procedure is **an Indian innovation** which combines asking a question with supplementaries and making brief comments; the Government also gets adequate opportunity to state its case. **The Calling Attention matter is not subject to the vote of** **the House. So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Adjournment Motion:** It is the procedure for adjournment of the business of the House for the purpose of discussing a definite matter of urgent public importance, which can be moved with the consent of the Speaker. The Adjournment Motion, **if admitted, leads to setting aside of the normal** **business of the House for discussing the matter mentioned in the motion**. To be in order, an adjournment motion must raise a matter of sufficient public importance to warrant interruption of normal business of the House and the question of public importance is decided on merit in each individual case. The purpose of an Adjournment Motion is to take the Government to task for a recent act of omission or commission having serious consequences. **Its adoption is regarded as a sort of censure** **of the Government**. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Censure Motion:** It is moved against the Government censuring its policy in some direction or an individual minister or ministers of the Government. **If it is passed in the Lok Sabha, the ministers** **need not resign from the office.** **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Zero Hour:** The time immediately following the Question Hour and laying of papers and before any listed business is taken up in the House has come to be popularly known as the `Zero Hour'. The term `Zero Hour' is not formally recognised in our parliamentary procedure. It is an informal device available to the members of the Parliament to **raise matters. So, pair IV is correctly matched.**
○ At present, twenty matters per day as per their priority in the ballot are allowed to be raised during "Zero Hour". The order in which the matters will be raised is decided by the Speaker at his/her discretion.
● **Special Mention:**  A matter which is not a point of order or which cannot be raised during question hour, half-an hour discussion, short duration discussion or under adjournment motion, calling attention notice or under any rule of the House can be raised under the special mention in the Rajya Sabha. Its equivalent procedural device in the Lok Sabha is known as ‘Notice (Mention) Under Rule 377’.  **So, pair** **V is not correctly matched.**
o A member can raise a **point of order** when the proceedings of the House do not follow the
normal rules of procedure. A point of order should relate to the interpretation or enforcement of the Rules of the House or such articles of the Constitution that regulate the business of the House and should raise a question that is within the cognizance of the Speaker. **It is usually raised by** **an opposition member in order to control the government.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Privileges of the Parliament:
I. - The Constitution of India provides for two privileges, freedom of speech in Parliament and the Right of Publication of its proceedings.
II. 	 The Presiding Officer of the House is the sole arbitrator to adjudge what constitutes the privilege of the Parliament.
III. 	 The Parliament can punish both the members and outsiders for the breach of its privilege.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Parliamentary privileges** are special rights and immunities enjoyed by the Houses and their members to ensure independent functioning. **Article 105** of the Constitution of India provides for the **powers,** **privileges, and immunities** of the Houses of Parliament, their members, and committees. The objective of this provision is to ensure the independent and efficient functioning of Parliament by protecting its members from undue interference.
● The **Constitution of India explicitly mentioned two privileges, that is, Freedom of Speech in** **Parliament and the Right of Publication of its proceedings.** With regard to other privileges, it provides that the Parliament by law will provide for what constitutes the Privileges of the Parliament by law, until then the privileges as they were at the time of commencement will continue. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
In the Sita Soren vs Union Of India, 2024 case the Supreme Court said that the Parliament is not the sole judge of privileges it enjoys and the **court can adjudicate** on the matter. **So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● Parliament holds **quasi-judicial powers** in matters related to its privileges, allowing it to **act against** **any individual member or non-member who obstructs or disrespects its functioning.** Parliament can impose punishments ranging from reprimand to imprisonment depending on the severity. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, only two of the above given statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Controller General of Accounts
II. 	 National Council for Transgender Persons
III. 	 NITI Aayog
IV. 	 Central Vigilance Commission
How many of the above are statutory bodies/ offices?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Statutory bodies** are institutions established by an Act of Parliament or State Legislatures, deriving their authority from legislation rather than directly from the Constitution as against the **Constitutional** **bodies** which are institutions or authorities whose powers, duties, and structures are explicitly defined in the Constitution.
● Apart from these there are **Executive bodies** as well, which are formed by the resolution of the Executive and don't have a Constitutional or Statutory backing.
● **Controller General of Accounts (CGA):** ○ It serves as the **Principal Advisor on accounting matters** to the Union Government. The CGA is responsible for establishing and maintaining a **technically sound management** **accounting system,** ensuring effective financial reporting and control. ○ Additionally, the CGA oversees the preparation and submission of the accounts of the Union Government, and plays a key role in **exchequer control and conducting internal audits** to uphold fiscal discipline and transparency within government operations. ○ It functions under the **Department of Expenditure, Ministry of Finance.** ○ It is a **non-statutory office.** **So, point I is not correct.**
● **National Council for Transgender Persons (NCTP):** ○ It is a **statutory body** constituted under the **Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights)** **Act 2019** to safeguard the rights of transgender persons in India. **So, point II is correct.** ○ The NCTP is responsible for monitoring and evaluating the **implementation of the provisions** **of the Act** and making recommendations to the government on measures to promote and protect the rights of transgender persons.
● **National Institution for Transforming India (NITI Aayog):** ○ It was established in **2015**, **replacing the** **erstwhile** **Planning Commission**, which had been in existence since 1950. ○ Its primary aim is to foster **cooperative federalism, promote bottom-up planning, and** **serve as a policy think tank** that provides strategic and technical advice to the Central and State Governments. NITI Aayog focuses on long-term development strategies, innovation, and inclusive growth, rather than centralized five-year plans.
○ It is a **non-constitutional non-statutory body,** created through an **executive resolution** **of the Union Government**. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Central Vigilance Commission (CVC):** ○ It is the premier Governmental agency for **preventing corruption in the Central** **Government**. It was established in **1964 by an executive resolution** of the Central government. Its establishment was recommended by **the Santhanam Committee on** **Prevention of Corruption** **(1962–64).** ○ Thus, originally the CVC was neither a constitutional body nor a statutory body. Later, in 2003, the Parliament enacted a law conferring **statutory status** on the CVC through **the Central** **Vigilance Commission Act, 2003**. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, only two of the above bodies/offices are statutory.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Important constitutional bodies in India:**
● Election Commission of India
● Union Public Service Commission (UPSC)
● State Public Service Commissions (SPSC)
● Finance Commission of India
● Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG)
● Attorney General of India
● Advocate General of State
● National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC)
● National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (NCST)
● National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC)
● Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities
● Goods and Services Tax (GST) Council
● State Finance Commission
● State Election Commission
● Inter-State Council **Important statutory bodies in India:**
● Reserve Bank of India (RBI)
● Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
● National Green Tribunal (NGT)
● Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI)
● Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA)
● Central Information Commission (CIC)
● Central Vigilance Commission (CVC)
● University Grants Commission (UGC)
● National Commission for Women (NCW)
● Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI)
● Competition Commission of India (CCI)
● Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)
● National Commission for Protection of Child Rights (NCPCR)

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Lok Sabha:
I. - A member can be suspended from the Lok Sabha only after a motion is passed in the House to that effect.
II. 	 Once suspended the suspension of a member can be revoked at will by the Speaker.
III. 	 The Speaker can ask for any member to withdraw from the House.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Speaker of the Lok Sabha and the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha** are responsible for maintaining order in Parliament to ensure its smooth functioning. They are empowered to direct disorderly members to **withdraw from the House for the day** or in cases **suspend them for the** **entire session** according to the provisions granted in Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business.
o It is to be noted here that withdrawal and suspension are not the same, withdrawal is when one
member is asked to leave the House for the remainder of that day’s session, while suspension is for the remainder of the session.
● For **suspension of members in Lok Sabha,** the speaker under **Rule 374 of the ‘Rules and** **procedure of Lok Sabha’** can name a member and present a motion for the suspension of the particular member or members. **The motion on being passed by the House, results in the** **suspension of the member.**
● **The authority to revoke that suspension lies with the House.** In such cases, the House can revoke the suspension by passing a resolution through a motion. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● In 2001, the Lok Sabha rules were amended to give the speaker one additional power. A new **Rule** **374A** was added, which allowed for **automatic suspension (without a motion) of a member** **named by the Speaker for grave disorder** (e.g., storming the Well, shouting slogans), for 5 sittings or the rest of the session, whichever is less. Hence, **the Lok Sabha speaker does not have to** **necessarily put forth a motion to suspend a member. So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Rule 373** of the Rules of procedure gives power to the Speaker for **directing a member to** **withdraw immediately for grossly disorderly conduct.** **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Suspension of MPs in Rajya Sabha:**
● **Rule 255:** The Chairman of Rajya Sabha can direct any member whose conduct is in his opinion grossly disorderly to withdraw immediately from the House.
● **Rule 256:** Under this rule, the Chairman may name a member for wilfully obstructing business, and the House can then suspend the Member for the rest of the session through a motion.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Which of the following parliamentary devices can be introduced only in Lok Sabha?
I. - Censure motion
II. 	 Adjournment Motion
III. 	 Privilege Motion
IV. 	 No Confidence Motion
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A motion in Parliament is a formal proposal made by a **member to draw the attention of the House** **to a particular issue.** It serves as a vital tool for ensuring government accountability and facilitating legislative scrutiny.  There are various motions to serve different purposes, and **some are exclusive** **to the Lok Sabha, where executive accountability lies.**
● **Censure Motion**: It is a formal resolution moved by the **Opposition** in the **Lok Sabha** to express **strong disapproval** of the policies or actions of the **Council of Ministers** or an **individual Minister**. Censure Motion can **only be introduced in the Lok Sabha**, as the **Council of Ministers is** **collectively responsible to the Lower House** under **Article 75(3)** of the Constitution. **So, point** **I is correct.**
o This motion must be **Specific and self-contained**, clearly stating the **grounds of criticism**.
This distinguishes it from a No-Confidence Motion, which does not require any reasons to be stated.
o If a Censure Motion is **passed**, the government is **not constitutionally bound to resign**.
However, it is **politically obligated to seek the confidence of the House** at the earliest opportunity.
● **Adjournment Motion:** It is a parliamentary device used to draw attention to a **specific, urgent,** **recent matter** **of** **public importance** **by** **interrupting the normal business** of the House. It is allowed **only in Lok Sabha** and requires the support of at least **50 members** to be admitted. **So,** **point II is correct**
● **No-Confidence Motion:** It is a motion that **tests whether the Council of Ministers continues** **to enjoy the confidence** of the Lok Sabha. The motion can be **introduced only in Lok Sabha** and requires the support of at least 50 members to be admitted. If passed by a **simple majority, the entire** **Council of Ministers must resign**. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Privilege Motion:** It is moved when there **is a breach of privilege** or contempt of the House or its members. Parliamentary privileges are granted under Article 105 to both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha. **Hence,** **privilege motions can be initiated in either House. So, point III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The Constitution of India does not specify
any time limit for the President to give his/
her assent to a bill.
II. 	 The President of India does not enjoy absolute
veto over the bills passed by the Parliament.
III. 	 The President of India does not have the
power of a qualified Veto.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A bill passed by the Parliament can become an act only if it receives the assent of the President. **Article** **111 of the Constitution of India** says that “When a Bill has been passed by the Houses of Parliament, it shall be presented to the President and the President shall declare either that he assents to the Bill, or that he withholds assent.” It further says that “Provided that the President may return the Bill to the Houses with a message requesting that they will reconsider the Bill.”
● Thus, when a bill is sent to the President after it has been passed by the Parliament, he has three options: ○ He may give his assent to the bill, or ○ He may withhold his assent to the bill, or
○ He may return the bill (if it is not a money bill) for reconsideration of the Parliament (if Parliament sends it back to the President, he/she has no choice but to approve it)
● The Constitution **does not specify any specified time limit** for the President to approve a Bill. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
○ The power of the President to not take any action on the bill but simply keeps the bill pending for an indefinite period is known as the **pocket veto**. The President can exercise this veto power as the Constitution does not prescribe any time-limit within which he has to take the decision with respect to a bill presented to him for his assent.
● **Absolute Veto** refers to the power of the President to withhold his assent to a bill passed by the Parliament. The bill then ends and does not become an act. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Qualified veto** refers to the veto powers which **can be overridden and conditional**, in this kind of veto, the condition for the sustenance is that if the bill which is vetoed by the President gets passed from the Legislature with a higher majority than it had for in the earlier instance, the veto gets overridden. **The President of India, doesn’t have any qualified veto. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Fixed tenure of six years or up to the age of
65 years
II. 	 Removal only in the manner of the judge of
the Supreme Court
III. 	 Not eligible for reappointment in the Central
or the State Government
IV. 	 Salary equivalent to the judge of the Supreme
Court
How many of the above provisions are made
in the Constitution of India for the office of the
Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 148** of the Constitution of India provides for an independent office of the **Comptroller and** **Auditor General of India (CAG).** H/ she is the head of the Indian Audit and Accounts Department. H/she is the guardian of the public purse and controls the entire financial system of the country at both the levels–the Centre and the state.
● **Provisions of Article 148 of the Constitution of India:** ○ Article 148 states that, the CAG shall be appointed by the **President by warrant under his** **hand and seal** and shall only be removed from office in like manner and on like **grounds as a** **Judge of the Supreme Court. So, point II is correct.** ○ Every person appointed to be the CAG shall, before he enters upon his office, make and subscribe before the President or some person appointed in that behalf by him, an oath or affirmation according to the form set out for the purpose in the Third Schedule. ○ The CAG **shall not be eligible for further office** either under the Government of India or under the Government of any State after he has ceased to hold his office. **So, point III is** **correct.** ○ The administrative expenses of the office of the CAG including all salaries, allowances and pensions payable to or in respect of persons serving in that office, shall be charged upon the Consolidated Fund of India.
● Further, Article 149 **authorizes the Parliament** to prescribe the duties and powers of the CAG in relation to the accounts of the Union and of the states and of any other authority or body. Accordingly, the Parliament enacted the **CAG’s (Duties, Powers and Conditions of Service) Act, 1971.**
● The Act states that, **the CAG shall hold office for a term of six years** from the date on which he assumes such office or till he attains the age of sixty-five years, whichever is earlier. **So, point I is not** **correct.**
● Also, Article 148 states that, **the salary and other conditions of service of the CAG shall be such** **as may be determined by Parliament by law** and, until they are so determined, shall be as specified in the Second Schedule: **As per the CAG (DPC) Act, 1971 the salary of the CAG is equal to** **the salary of the Judge of the Supreme Court. So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only two of the above provisions are made in the Constitution of India for the office of the** **Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG).**
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Other  provisions to safeguard and ensure the independence of CAG:**
● The conditions of service of persons serving in the **Indian Audit and Accounts** **Department** and the administrative powers of the CAG are prescribed by the president after consultation with the CAG.
● The **administrative expenses of the office of the CAG**, including all salaries, allowances and pensions of persons serving in that office are charged upon the Consolidated Fund of India. Thus, they are not subject to the vote of Parliament.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Anti Defection Law:
I. - The Presiding Officer of the House can take suo moto cognisance of the defection.
II. 	 There is no time limit for the presiding officer to decide on the disqualification proceedings as per Schedule X.
III. 	 In the Kihoto Hollohan case (1992), the Supreme Court held that the decision of the Presiding Officer regarding disqualification is subject to judicial review.
Which of the statements given above is/are not correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The 52nd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1985** introduced the anti-defection law by adding the **Tenth Schedule** to the Constitution. Under this provision, a member of the Parliament or State Legislature can **be disqualified if they voluntarily give up their party membership** or vote (or abstain from voting) against the party’s directive.
● The anti-defection law **allows any member of the House** to initiate the process of disqualification by filing a petition/complaint with the Speaker (Lok Sabha) or Chairman (Rajya Sabha) alleging defection by another member. The **Presiding Officer of the House cannot take suo moto cognisance** of the defection. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The Speaker of Lok Sabha, the Chairman of Rajya Sabha or the State Legislative Assembly is the deciding authority who decides disqualification petitions under the anti-defection law.  However, the **law does** **not specify a strict timeline** for the decision. The Presiding Officers can decide on the case without any time limits. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Supreme Court in **Kihoto Hollohan vs. Zachillhu and Others (1992)** said that the decisions of the Speaker or Chairman regarding disqualification are **subject to judicial review to ensure a** **system of checks and balances**. However, judicial intervention is limited to cases involving malafide intention, perversity, or violation of natural justice. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Party Whip:**
● A whip is a member of the parliamentary party nominated by the political party in the House. The whip is responsible for giving the voting instructions to the party members. **Anti-defection law for Nominated members:**
● As per the anti-defection law, the nominated members in Rajya Sabha can join a political party within six months of their nomination.
● But if they join a party after the first six months, they may face disqualification from the Rajya Sabha. **Exceptions:**
● **The law permits mergers of political parties when two-thirds of the** **members of a legislative party** agree to merge with another party. In these instances, legislators are not considered to have defected. Earlier, there **was no** **disqualification of members if one third members** of a party split to form a separate group. But it **was deleted by the 91****st** **amendment act.**
● However, these instances paved the way for conflict over the allocation of party symbols and names to the conflicting factions which was resolved by the Supreme Court’s Three Test Formula. **Supreme Court’s Three Test Formula:**
● The **Supreme Court in Sadiq Ali versus Election Commission of India** **(1971)** laid down the three-test formula for deciding which faction is to be recognised as the original political party by the Election Commission.
● The three fundamental tests are o the test of aims and objectives of the party, o the test of party constitution, which reflects inner-party democracy, and o the test of majority in the legislative and organisational wings In the **Ravi S. Naik vs Union of India (1994)** and other judgments, **the Supreme Court** **held that even without a formal resignation, conduct implying allegiance to** **another party can lead to disqualification.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText: For the purpose of Article 83 of the Constitution of India, which one of the following is taken as the initiation date of the newly constituted Lok Sabha?

options.a: Day of the result declaration for general election
options.b: Day of the oath taking of the Council of Ministers
options.c: Day of the first sitting of the Lok Sabha
options.d: Day of the assumption of office by the Speaker

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 83(2) of the Constitution of India** and Section 14 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951 **provides for the conduct of the elections to constitute a new Lok Sabha before the** **expiry of its current term**.
● For the purpose of calculating the five year period**, it is the date of the first sitting of the Lok** **Sabha which is taken to be the initiation date.**
● For example, for the 17th Lok Sabha the first sitting took place on 17th June 2019, therefore its term expired on 16th June 2024, and elections for the 18th Lok Sabha were concluded on 4th June 2024. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to election petitions in India:
I. - The High Courts have original jurisdiction on deciding the election petition.
II. 	 They have to be submitted within thirty days of the declaration of result.
III. 	 They need to be mandatorily disposed of within one year of its filing.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Election petition is a procedure for inquiring into the validity of the election results of** **Parliamentary or local government elections**. In other words, it is a means under law to challenge the election of a candidate in a Parliamentary, Assembly or local election.
● Election petitions are filed in the High Court of the particular state in which the election was conducted. Therefore, **only the High Courts have the original jurisdiction on deciding on election** **petitions**. Such jurisdiction shall be exercised ordinarily by the Single Judge of the High Court and Chief Justice shall from time to time assign one or more Judges for that purpose. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Under Section 81(1) of the RPA, 1951, **an election petition must be filed within 45 days from** **the date of the declaration of the result.** If the petition is submitted even one day after the 45-day window, it is liable to be rejected as barred by limitation. This statutory time frame is strictly enforced to ensure timely redressal. The intent is to resolve election disputes efficiently without prolonging uncertainty. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Sub-section (7) of section 86 of the Representation of the People Act (RPA), 1951 provides** **that every election petition shall be tried as expeditiously as possible** and endeavour shall be made to **conclude the trial within six months** from the date on which the election petition is presented to the High Court for trial. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005:
I. - It has an overriding effect over the provisions of the Official Secrets Act, 1923.
II. 	 It is applicable to all constitutional authorities except the judiciary.
III. 	 Personal details of officials can be accessed under the RTI in public interest.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005** mandates timely response to citizen requests for government information. The basic object of the act is to **empower the citizens, promote** **transparency and accountability** in the working of the Government, contain corruption, and make our democracy work for the people in a real sense.
● The **Official Secrets Act, 1923 (OSA)** is a colonial era law brought into maintaining the secrecy of Government’s functioning, under this Act the Government officers are not liable to disclose information related to their functioning to the public.
● **Section 22 of the RTI Act** provides for the primacy of the RTI Act vis-a-vis provisions of other laws,
including OSA. This gives the RTI Act an **overriding effect, notwithstanding anything inconsistent**
**with the provisions of OSA.** So, if there is any inconsistency in OSA with regard to furnishing of
information, it will be **superseded by the RTI Act. So, statement I is correct.**
● The RTI Act is **applicable to all constitutional authorities**, **including the executive, legislature**
**and judiciary**; **any institution or body established or constituted by an Act of Parliament or**
**a State Legislature.** It is an Act of the Parliament of India “to provide for setting out the practical
regime of right to information for citizens.” **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Under the RTI Act, it is **not mandatory** for the public authorities to disclose information, which relates to **personal information,** the disclosure of which has no relationship to any public activity or interest, or which would cause unwarranted invasion of the privacy of the individual (but it is also provided that the information which cannot be denied to the Parliament or a State Legislature shall not be denied by this exemption).
○ The Supreme Court of India in the matter of **Girish R. Deshpande vs. CIC and others had** ruled that the personal information of government officials could be disclosed, **only if it would** **serve a larger public interest.** **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Vigilance Commission (CVC):
I. - It advises the Central Government on the appointment of the Director of Prosecution in the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI).
II. 	 The Chief Vigilance Commissioner is a member of the committee that recommends candidates for appointment as the Director of the CBI.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) is the main agency for **preventing corruption in the** **Central government**.
● The CVC is a Multi-member Commission consisting of a **Central Vigilance Commissioner** **(Chairperson) and not more than two Vigilance Commissioners (Members).** The CVC has its **own Secretariat**, the Chief Technical Examiners’ Wing (CTE), and a wing of Commissioners for Departmental Inquiries (CDI).
○ The **Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013** has amended some provisions of the CVC Act, 2003,
which empowers the Commission to conduct preliminary inquiry into complaints referred by the Lokpal in respect of **officers and officials of Groups A, B, C & D,** for which a **Directorate** **of Inquiry** (for making preliminary inquiry) is to be set up in the Commission.
■ The Act mandates that appointment of **Director of Prosecution** by the Central
Government under the Directorate of Prosecution in **Central Bureau of** **Investigation (CBI)** to be done on the **recommendation of the CVC**. **So,** **statement I is correct.** ■ A **selection committee headed by the Central Vigilance Commissioner** that
includes the two vigilance commissioners, secretaries of M/o Home Affairs, and D/o Personnel and Training to recommend officers to the post of the level of Superintendent of Police and above in the CBI. **Appointment of CBI Director:**
● The **CVC Act, 2003** has fixed the tenure of the CBI director **at 2 years.**
● The CBI director is appointed according to provisions under **the Delhi Special Police** **Establishment Act (1946)** **and amendments** therein brought **by the Lokpal and** **Lokayuktas Act, 2013.**
● The appointment is **made by the Centre on recommendation** by a three-member Selection Committee composed of **the Prime Minister as the chairperson**, the **Leader of the** **Opposition (LoP) in the Lok Sabha** and the **Chief Justice of India, or a Supreme Court** **judge** nominated by him. If there is no recognised LoP, then **the leader of the single largest** **party** is included in the committee. **So, statement II is not correct.**
○ It must be noted here that CVC does sit on the selection panel of the Director of Enforcement Directorate. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The process of impeachment of the President of India is a quasi-judicial procedure.
**Statement II:** The Members of the concerned House of the Parliament act as the investigating agency during the impeachment process of the President.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **President can be removed from office by a process of impeachment for ‘violation of the** **Constitution’**. Article 61 explicitly uses the term impeachment and says that ‘When a President is to be impeached for violation of the Constitution, **the charge shall be preferred by either House of** **Parliament.’**
○ The term ‘impeachment’ in the Constitution of India is exclusively used for the President. For other constitutional functionaries like the Vice President, or the Judges, the term ‘removal’ is used.
● The impeachment charges on the President **can be initiated through a resolution in either House** **of Parliament**. When such a resolution has been passed by the House, it is referred to another House. The **other House investigates the charges** and then decides whether the charges preferred against the President have been sustained or not. **So, statement II is correct.**
● A **quasi-judicial procedure is one in which the Members of the House are acting as judges** **rather than legislators**; the House is not making new laws, but rather applying existing laws to specific facts. So, the impeachment process involves investigation, presentation of the evidence, hearing the parties and making a decision similar to the judicial process. **So, statement I is correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI):
I. - It functions under the Department of Personnel and Training.
II. 	 The Director of CBI enjoys security of tenure.
III. 	 It coordinates investigations on behalf of Interpol Member countries.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)**, functioning under the **Department of Personnel,** **Ministry of Personnel, Pension & Public Grievances, Government of India**, is the premier investigating police agency in India. It is a non-statutory executive body, established under the Delhi Police Special Establishment Act, 1946. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Director of the CBI is appointed by the Government on the basis of recommendations from a collegium consisting of the Prime Minister, Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha, **Chief Justice of India** **or any Supreme Court judge nominated by him/her.** It must be noted here that, this provision of selection by a select committee is provided under the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013 (as amended in 2014).
● The Director of CBI was provided a **secured tenure of two years via the Central Vigilance** **Commission Act, 2003.** This was post the verdict of the Supreme Court in the **Vineet Narain versus** **Union of India case**, **1997** wherein the court issued a specific order that the CBI director shall have a minimum tenure of two years. **So, statement II is correct.**
o As recently in the year 2021, the Parliament via the Central Vigilance Commission (Amendment)
Act, 2021 and the Delhi Special Police Establishment (Amendment) Act, 2021, provided that this **two-year tenure of the CBI director can be extended one-year at a time thrice, this** **effectively making the maximum tenure that a CBI director can serve to five years**. These amendments were subsequently upheld by the Supreme Court in 2023.
● CBI is an elite force playing a major role in preservation of values in public life and in ensuring the health of the national economy. It is also the nodal police agency in India, which **coordinates investigations** **on behalf of Interpol Member countries**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Subject:** Polity | Constitutional and Non Constitutional bodies **Tags:** Factual

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct with regard to the Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) of India?
I. - The Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha, and the Chief Justice of India constitute members of the panel for his selection.
II. 	 S/he is removed by following the same procedure as the removal of the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.
Select the correct answer using the code below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Chief Election Commissioner (CEC)** of India plays a crucial role in ensuring free and fair elections. Appointed by the President under **Article 324(2)** of the Constitution, the CEC holds a position of **critical importance in safeguarding** the democratic process.
● To ensure independence from executive interference, the CEC is granted certain constitutional protections, particularly regarding **security of tenure and removal**.
● Article **324(5)** safeguards the **independence** of the Election Commission by ensuring the CEC's security of tenure and protecting it from arbitrary removal.
● The **Election Commissioners (Appointment, Conditions of Service and Term of Office) Act, 2023** regulates the appointment of the CEC and the Election Commissioners (ECs). Earlier, the CEC and ECs were appointed by the President after recommendations of the government. Under the amendment, the **selection** **panel consists of the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha, and the Union** **Cabinet Minister nominated by the PM**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● CEC **cannot be removed from office except** in the manner and on the grounds prescribed for a **Supreme Court judge by a resolution passed by both Houses of Parliament** with a special majority, and on the grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity. **So, statement II is not correct.**
o However, the other Election Commissioners (ECs), unlike the CEC, do not enjoy the same level
of protection. They can be removed on the recommendation of the CEC. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following constitutional functionaries:
I. - Chief Election Commissioner
II. 	 Comptroller and Auditor General
III. 	 Attorney General
IV. 	 Chairman of the National Commission of Scheduled Castes
How many of the above can be removed on the grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Constitution of India provides detailed provisions for the **appointment and removal of key** **Constitutional functionaries** to ensure the independence and integrity of their offices. While some offices have clearly defined grounds and procedures for removal, others are left to the discretion of the executive, with no explicit constitutional grounds for removal.
● **Article 324(5) of the Constitution of India** states that the Chief Election Commissioner can only be removed in the same manner and on the **same grounds as a Supreme Court judge,** that is for **proved misbehaviour or incapacity. So, point I is correct.**
● According to **Article 148(1)** of the Constitution of India, the **Comptroller and Auditor General** **(CAG)** can be removed from office by the President, but on the grounds of **proved misbehaviour or** **incapacity**, and following the same procedure as that for removing a Supreme Court judge. **So, point** **II is correct.**
● **Article 76 of the Constitution of India** establishes the office of **the Attorney General (AG)** and outlines the President's power to appoint them, but it does not specify the grounds or procedure for their removal. The Constitution states that the AG holds office during the pleasure of the President. **So,** **point IV is not correct.**
● **The National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC)** is a constitutional body under Article
338. While the Constitution outlines its establishment and functions, **the specific grounds for** **removing its chairman and the process involved are not detailed in the Constitution itself**. **So, point V is not correct.**
o The NCSC consists of a chairperson, a vice-chairperson and three other members. They are
appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal. Their conditions of service and tenure of office are also determined by the President **So, only two of the above can be disqualified on the grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Union Public Service Commission (UPSC):
I. - The Constitution does not prescribe a fixed strength for the Commission.
II. 	 At least half of the UPSC members must have held office for a minimum of ten years under either the Government of India or a State Government.
III. 	 The Chairman of the UPSC is not eligible for any further employment under the Government of India or a State Government.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Union Public Service Commission (UPSC)** is a Constitutional body established under Article 315 of the Constitution of India. It serves as the central recruiting agency, responsible for conducting examinations and selecting candidates for various All India Services, Central Services, and Group A and B posts within the Government of India.
● The UPSC comprises a **Chairman and other members** who are appointed by the President of India. The Constitution does **not prescribe a fixed strength for the Commission**, **leaving its** **composition to be determined at the President’s discretion. So, statement I is correct.**
● The Constitution lays down singular qualifications for members of the UPSC that **at least half of the** **members must have held office for a minimum of ten years under either the Government** **of India or a State Government. So, statement II is correct.**
● The Constitution empowers the **President to determine the service conditions of the Chairman** **and members of the UPSC.** They hold office for a term of six years or until they reach the age of 65 years, whichever is earlier.
● On ceasing to hold office, the Chairman of the UPSC is not eligible for any further employment under the Government of India or a State Government**. A member of the UPSC, however, may be** **appointed as the Chairman** of the UPSC or as the Chairman of a State Public Service Commission, but **is not eligible for any other government employment** at the Centre or in the States, hence the members of UPSC cannot be reappointed for the same post the expiry of their term. **So, statement** **III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText: In a situation when the offices of both the President and the Vice President are vacant at the same time, then as per the Constitution of India, who amongst the following will discharge the functions of the President of India?

options.b: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha
options.c: The Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 65 in the Constitution of India makes Vice-President to act as President** or to discharge his functions during casual vacancies in the office, or during the absence of the President.
○ The Vice-President acts as President during casual vacancy in the office of the President by reason of death, resignation or removal or otherwise when the President is unable to discharge his functions owing to absence, illness or any other cause, the Vice-President discharges those functions till the President resumes office.
● However, the **Constitution makes no provisions** regarding who will act as the President of India when the posts of President as well as Vice-President fall vacant at the same time.
● This exigency has been dealt with by **the President (Discharge of Functions) Act, 1969 which** **provides that in case** the offices of both the President and Vice-President falls vacant, **the Chief** **Justice of India** or, in his absence, the senior-most Judge of the Supreme Court of India available **shall** **discharge the functions of the President until a new President is elected.**
● **Mohammad Hidayatullah** is the only Chief Justice of India who acted as a President of India when President Dr. Zakir Hussain died and then Vice-President V.V. Giri resigned from his post to contest the Presidential election prior to the 1969 presidential election. The Parliament at that time passed the above mentioned legislation. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With regard to the Lok Sabha, consider the following statements:
I. - All the members of the Lok Sabha are directly elected on the basis of Universal Adult Suffrage.
II. 	 Fourth Schedule of the Constitution provides for the allocation of Lok Sabha seats among the States.
III. 	 The Constitution (106th Amendment) Act of 2023, reserved one-third of all seats for women in Lok Sabha.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Lok Sabha is the Lower House of the Indian Parliament and consists of directly elected legislators from all the States and Union Territories of the Indian union. The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha is fixed at 550 (31st Constitutional Amendment Act, 1972). At present there are 543 seats in the Lok Sabha.
● **Presently all the 543 members of the Lok Sabha are elected directly by the people, making** **it a completely elected House**. Earlier it was a partly elected and partly nominated body (prior to 104th Constitutional Amendment in 2019, the Lok Sabha also had provisions for nominated members, the President could nominate two members of Anglo-Indian community, if in her opinion the community was not adequately represented in the House. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Fourth Schedule of the Constitution provides for allocation of seats to States in the Council of States (**Rajya Sabha**). **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **The Constitution (106th Amendment) Act of 2023, reserves one-third of all seats for** **women in Lok Sabha**, State legislative assemblies, and the Legislative Assembly of the National Capital Territory of Delhi. **So, statement III is correct.**
○ As per the provisions in the Act, the reservation of one-third of seats for women in the Lok Sabha and the Assemblies shall come into effect after the delimitation exercise is taken based on the figures from the first Census conducted after the enactment of the Act. The next Census will be held by 1 March 2027. So, the women's quota in the Lok Sabha will roll out after that. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following situations:
I. - If he/she commits a breach of a parliamentary
privilege
II. 	 If he/she is elected to the State Legislative
Assembly
III. 	 If he/she is convicted of a criminal offence
with imprisonment for more than two years
IV. 	 If he/she disobeys a three-line whip issued by
his political party
In how many of the above situations does a Member
of Parliament get disqualified immediately from
his/her seat in the Parliament?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The disqualification of the legislators in Parliament is governed under the Rules and Procedure of the Parliament, Representation of People’s Act, 1951, the Tenth Schedule amongst others Legislative and Constitutional provisions.
● If any individual or authority **violates or disregards any of the privileges**, powers and immunities of the House or members or committees thereof, he may be punished for “breach of privilege.” A person or member found guilty of breach of privilege of the House may be punished either by **imprisonment,** **or by admonition (warning) or reprimand,** two other punishments may also be awarded to the members namely, ‘suspension’ and ‘expulsion’ from the House. But any of this punishment will ensue only after the charges against the member are proved in a quasi-judicial inquiry of the House or any of its committee, **further for any such punishment a motion needs to be passed by the House.** **So, point I is not correct.**
● According to **Article 101 of the Constitution, a person cannot be a member of both the** **Parliament and the State Legislature at the same time.** If a person is so elected, his seat in Parliament becomes vacant if **he/she does not resign his seat in the State Legislature within 14** **days.** **So, point II is not correct.**
● Under the Representation of People Act (1951), a person shall be disqualified for being elected as a member of Parliament **if he has been convicted for any offence resulting in imprisonment for** **two or more years.** In such a scenario **the court order for conviction is deemed as the** **disqualification order for the particular legislator**, also there is no need for the official notification from the Parliamentary Secretariat for the same. **So, point III is correct.**
● A whip is a written order that a political party issues to its members for being present for an important vote, or that they vote only in a particular way. The members are supposed to follow the directives given by the whip. Otherwise, disciplinary action can be taken. **The Three-line whip is issued to members** **directing them to vote as per the party line**. It is the strictest of all the whip. If a legislator violates his party’s whip, he faces disqualification from the House under the Anti Defection law. The AntiDefection Law says that a member of a House belonging to any political party **shall be disqualified for** **being a member of the House if he votes or abstains from voting** contrary to direction issued by his political party. However, **the disqualification is not immediate.** If the party concerned condones the action of its legislator within fifteen days of any such actions, no disqualification proceedings are initiated. **So, point IV is not correct.** **So, in only one of the above situations does a Member of the Parliament immediately get** **disqualified from his/her seat in the Parliament.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With regard to the Speaker of Lok Sabha, consider
the following statements:
I. - She/he holds the office during the pleasure of
the President.
II. 	 She/he cannot vote on any motion or
resolution in the Lok Sabha.
III. 	 Only she/he can prorogue a session of the
Parliament.
How many of the statements given above are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● As per **Article 93** of the Constitution of India, the members of the Lok Sabha may elect the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker from among its members. According to the Article, Speaker and Deputy Speaker are to be **elected “as soon as may be” after the commencement of the House.** The Speaker is elected by a simple majority in the House.
● Usually, the Speaker remains in office during the life of the Lok Sabha. **The Speaker may be removed** **by a resolution passed by a majority of all the then members of the Lok Sabha.** Such a resolution can be moved only after giving 14 days’ advance notice. His tenure does not depend on the pleasure of the President. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **The Speaker is allowed to give his/her casting vote**. According to Article 100 of the Constitution,
which talks about voting in the Houses, the Chairman of Rajya Sabha or Speaker of Lok Sabha, or any person acting as such, **“shall not vote in the first instance, but shall exercise a casting vote in** **the case of any equality of votes**”. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Termination of a session of the House by an order of the President is called ‘prorogation’. Under Article 85(2) of the Constitution, **the President may from time to time prorogue sessions in either** **House of Parliament.** **So, statement III is not correct.**
○ It must be noted here that, Speaker can adjourn the sitting of the Lok Sabha. **So, none of the statements given above are correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Union Government, consider the following pairs:
Cabinet Committee Chairperson
I. - Cabinet Committee on Accommodation Prime Minister
II. - Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs Home Minister
III. - Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs Finance Minister
IV. - Cabinet Committee on Security Defence Minister
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Cabinet Committees are set up by the Prime Minister with selected members of the** **Cabinet. He assigns specific functions to these committees.** The Prime Minister may change the numbers of committees, and modify the functions assigned to them.
● The membership of each committee varies from three to eight. Usually, only Cabinet ministers are members of these committees. But, non-Cabinet ministers can be the members or special invitees to committees. If the Prime Minister himself is a member of any such committee, he acts as the head of that committee.
● The committees resolve issues and formulate proposals for the consideration of the Cabinet and take decisions on matters assigned to them.
● There are **eight Cabinet committees at present** — the Appointments Committee of the Cabinet, Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs, Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs, Cabinet Committee on Investment and Growth, Cabinet Committee on Security, Cabinet Committee on Parliamentary Affairs, Cabinet Committee on Skill, Employment and Livelihood, and Cabinet Committee on Accommodation.
● **Cabinet Committee on Accommodation:** It is a specialized committee within the Indian government responsible for overseeing matters related to the allocation of government accommodation. This committee deals primarily with the allocation of residential and official accommodation to ministers, senior officials, and other government functionaries. This committee is chaired by the **Union Home** **Minister.** **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs:** It handles issues involving relations between the national and state governments, examines economic and societal matters requiring a comprehensive view, and addresses international matters lacking internal or external security repercussions. Known also as the 'super cabinet,' its assemblage incorporates most of the principal Cabinet ministers. This committee is chaired by the **Prime Minister** himself. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs:** It is a key decision-making body within the Indian government, primarily responsible for handling economic policy matters. It plays a crucial role in formulating and implementing economic strategies, managing financial resources, and overseeing major economic projects and reforms. This committee is chaired by the **Prime Minister** himself. **So, pair III** **is not correctly matched.**
● **Cabinet Committee on Security:** It is led by the **Prime Minister** and includes the Ministers of Defence, Home Affairs, Finance, and External Affairs. Its key responsibilities include shaping national security and defense policies, overseeing defense procurement, and guiding the country's strategic posture, particularly in nuclear and missile programs. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Attorney General (AG) for India:
I. - He must be eligible to be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court.
II. 	 He enjoys all the privileges and immunities that are available to a Member of Parliament.
III. 	 He is not prohibited from taking up private cases during his term of office.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 76 of the Constitution of India** has provided for the office of the **Attorney General for** **India**. He is the **highest law officer** in the country. The Attorney General (AG) is **appointed by the** **President**. Any person who is as eligible to become a **judge of the Supreme Court** can be appointed as the AG. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ The term of office of the AG is **not fixed** by the Constitution. Further, the **Constitution does** **not contain** the procedure and grounds for his removal. He holds office during the **pleasure** **of the president.** This means that he may be removed by the **president at any time**. ○ He may also quit his office by submitting his **resignation to the president**. Conventionally, he resigns when the government (council of ministers) resigns or is replaced, as he is appointed on its advice.
● He enjoys all the **privileges and immunities that are available to a member of Parliament**.
Further, he has the right to speak and to take part in the proceedings of both the Houses of Parliament
or their joint sitting and any committee of the Parliament of which he may be named a member, but
**without a right to vote. So, statement II is correct.**
● The AG is **not a fulltime counsel** for the Government. He does not fall in the category of government
servants. Further, he is **not barred from private legal practice** during his term of office. **So,**
**statement III is correct.**
● The remuneration of the AG is **not fixed by the Constitution**. He receives such remuneration as the
President may determine and his remuneration is not constitutionally protected from reduction during
tenure.
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **Functions of the Attorney General of India:**
● As the chief law officer of the Government of India, the duties of the AG include the following:
○ To give **advice to the Government of India** upon such legal matters,
which are referred to him by the president. ○ To perform such **other duties of a legal character** that are assigned to him by the president. ○ To **discharge the functions** conferred on him by the Constitution or any other law. ○ The president has assigned the following duties to the AG: ○ To **appear on behalf of the Government of India** in all cases in the Supreme Court in which the Government of India is concerned. ○ To **represent the Government of India** in any reference made by the president to the Supreme Court under **Article 143 of the Constitution.** ○ To appear (when required by the Government of India) **in any high court** **in any case** in which the Government of India is concerned.
● In the performance of his official duties, the Attorney General has the **right of** **audience in all courts** in the territory of India.
● Following **limitations** are placed on the Attorney General in order to avoid any complication and conflict of duty:
○ He should not advise or hold a brief **against the Government of India**. ○ He should not advise or hold a brief in cases in which he is called upon to advise or appear for the Government of India. ○ He should **not defend accused persons in criminal prosecutions** without the permission of the Government of India. ○ He should **not accept appointment as a director in any company** **or corporation** without the permission of the Government of India. ○ He should **not advise any ministry or department of Government** **of India** or any statutory organization or any public sector undertaking unless the proposal or a reference in this regard is received through the Ministry of Law and Justice, Department of Legal Affairs.

questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the enactment of the Union Budget, which of the following powers are **not** granted to the Rajya Sabha?
I. - General discussion on the budget
II. 	 Voting on demand for grants
III. 	 Amendment to the appropriation bill
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Union Budget** in India is a constitutional and procedural exercise carried out annually by the central government.
● While both Houses of Parliament are involved in the broader budget process, **certain stages are** **exclusive to the Lok Sabha** due to its financial prerogatives.
