● The stages of the General Budget include: o **Presentation of Budget:** The presentation of the Union Budget **happens in the Lok Sabha.**
It begins with the **Finance Minister’s speech**, providing a summary of the economic performance in the previous and current financial years, along with the government's expenditure plans for the upcoming year. It also contains the **tax proposals** for the coming financial year. In the **Rajya Sabha**, there is no budget speech. Instead, a **junior minister from the Finance** **Ministry** simply lays down the budget documents. o **General Discussion:** The Chairman appoints the day and **time for general discussion on**
**the Budget in Rajya Sabha.** The House is at liberty to discuss the Budget as a whole or any question of principle involved therein, but no motion is moved nor the Budget is submitted to the vote of Rajya Sabha. **So, point I is not correct.**
▪ This stage involves a broad examination of the Budget without going into details of allocations or tax proposals. Members discuss overall fiscal policy, government priorities, and administrative performance. o **Scrutiny by Committees:** After the General Discussion ends, both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha
are adjourned for 3–4 weeks. The 24 Department Related Standing Committees in Parliament examine the budget demands (Demands for Grants) of specific ministries. They discuss these in detail, prepare reports, and submit them to both Houses for consideration. o **Voting on Demands for Grants:** Voting on Demands for Grants is **exclusive to the Lok**
**Sabha**; the **Rajya Sabha has no voting powers in this matter.** Based on the reports of the Departmental Standing Committees, the Lok Sabha proceeds to vote on the Demands for Grants during the budget process. A Demand for Grant becomes a Grant only after it is duly voted upon by the Lok Sabha. **So, point II is correct.** o **Passing of Appropriation Bill:** Once the Demands for Grants are passed by the Lok Sabha,
they are consolidated into the Appropriation Bill. This Bill seeks Parliament's approval to withdraw funds from the Consolidated Fund of India to meet both voted and charged expenditures. **Passing the bill provides legal authority to the government to spend** **money from the Consolidated Fund**. After being passed by the Lok Sabha, the bill is **transmitted to the Rajya Sabha** for their consideration. The **Rajya Sabha has no power** **to amend or reject** it. It can only **recommend changes**, and must return the bill **within 14** **days**. **So, point III is correct.** o **Passing of Finance Bill:** The Finance Bill containing the Government’s financial proposals like
**Taxation, Government borrowings and Expenditures from or receipts into the** **Consolidated Fund of India** for the upcoming financial year is presented in the Parliament. It seeks **approval only from the Lok Sabha**. Once passed, Rajya Sabha can make recommendations to it as approved by Lok Sabha. **Therefore, option (b) is the answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
No person who is in police custody at the time of
elections can contest elections.
**Statement II:**
No person who is in police custody at the time of
elections is allowed to vote.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct,
but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct, but Statement II is not
correct
options.d:
Statement I is not correct, but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The qualifications and disqualifications for contesting the elections are outlined in Articles 84, 102, 173, and 191 of the Constitution, as well as in the Representation of the People Act, 1951.
● **Under the Representation of the People Act**, if a person is convicted and sentenced to a jail term of not less than two years, he/she stands disqualified automatically from contesting elections for six years from the date of release from prison.
● The bar on contesting elections applies **only after a person has been convicted**. Persons who are under police custody during the time of elections can contest elections. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● According to Section 62(5) of the RP Act, **a person who is in jail or in police custody is not** **permitted to vote in any election**. This restriction extends to individuals who are serving a sentence after conviction, awaiting trial as an undertrial, or held in police custody even temporarily (such as during remand), or detained for any other reason. **So, statement II is correct.**
o However, this restriction **does not apply to a person under preventive detention** (as per
the proviso to Section 62(5)). Because preventive detention is not based on conviction or a charge. It is a precautionary measure and not considered punitive. He is entitled to vote by Postal Ballot paper.
● The Supreme Court in 2013 in a ruling stated that a person who is prohibited from voting due to being in police custody or in jail, as long as his/her name is present in the electoral role shall not cease to be an elector, and since the person is an elector, he/she can file nomination for an election. **So, statement I is not correct, but statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the None of The Above (NOTA) option available
in the elections:
I. - If the NOTA receives the highest number of
votes than any of the contesting candidates
then a re-election needs to be conducted.
II. 	 NOTA votes are not counted for the
calculation of total votes for the return of
security deposits to candidates.
III. 	 There is no provision of NOTA option in the
Rajya Sabha elections.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **None of The Above (NOTA)** is an option on the Electronic Voting Machine (EVM) or ballot that allows voters to reject all candidates contesting in an election. It expresses voter dissatisfaction without abstaining from voting. It was made mandatory by the Supreme Court in the case of **People’s Union for** **Civil Liberties (PUCL) Vs Union of India, 2013**. The Court held that **the right to vote also includes** **the right to reject** all candidates, as part of the freedom of expression under Article 19(1)(a).
● As per the rules framed by the Election Commission of India, the **votes polled under NOTA are** **part of the total votes polled in an election, but are not calculated under valid votes polled**, and for the result calculation purposes the latter parameter is taken as the consideration.
● That is why even if NOTA receives the highest number of votes, but a contesting candidate receives the second-highest, **that candidate can still be declared the winner**. This is considered as the major disadvantage of NOTA. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **According to Section 158 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951**, only the valid votes received by the contesting candidates are counted to determine if a candidate has secured enough votes to retain their deposit. A candidate must secure **at least one-sixth of the valid votes** to retain their deposit. **So, statement II is correct.**
● In **2018, the Supreme Court scrapped** the use of NOTA option in the Rajya Sabha elections. The court held that the **Rajya Sabha elections are indirect** and follow proportional representation through a single transferable vote under Article 80(4). Since MLAs vote via an **open ballot to maintain** **party discipline**, introducing NOTA would violate this system and go against the Supreme Court’s 2013 PUCL judgment, which allows NOTA only in direct elections. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, only two of the above statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Which of the following crimes come under the jurisdiction of the National Investigation Agency?
I. - Human trafficking
II. 	 Counterfeit currency
III. 	 Cyberterrorism
IV. 	 Sale of prohibited arms
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **National Investigation Agency (NIA)** was set up under the **National Investigation Agency** **Act, 2008** as a Central Counter Terrorism Law Enforcement Agency. **The** **jurisdiction of the agency** **extends to the whole of India** as per the NIA Act, 2008.
● The NIA is assigned cases by the **Ministry of Home Affairs** pertaining to offences provided in the schedule to the NIA Act, 2008, either on the basis of a report from the State Government or suo-motu based on the gravity of the offences as envisaged under Section 6 of the Act.
● In other words, the NIA can investigate those offences as mentioned under the schedule of the Act across the country both on **directions of the Central Government as well as  on** **suo moto** **basis.**
● It should be noted here that, the NIA doesn’t enjoy exclusive jurisdiction over any of these Acts, the crimes under them continue to be investigated as such by the respective investigative agencies at the State or the Central level, but if NIA or the Central Government factoring in the gravity of the situation wants to take over the case, then the agency have the primacy to do so.
● The **2008 Act provided jurisdiction to NIA over the Atomic Energy Act, 1962, and the** **Unlawful Activities Prevention Act, 1967.**  Later, an amendment in 2019 extended its jurisdiction over the offences of:
o **Human trafficking. So, point I is correct.** o **Counterfeit currency or bank notes. So, point II is correct.** o **Cyber-terrorism. So, point III is correct.** o **Manufacture or sale of prohibited arms. So, point IV is correct.** o Offences under the Explosive Substances Act, 1908. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Withholding assent to a State Bill which was reserved for her consideration
II. 	 Consulting the Supreme Court of India under Article 143 of the Constitution
III. 	 Appointment of Prime Minister when no party has a clear majority in the Lok Sabha In how many of the above situations can the President of India use her discretion?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● As per **Article 53** of the Constitution of India, the executive authority of the Union is vested with the President, when read with **Article 74** of the Constitution, these powers are to be exercised by the Presidential office in aid and advice of the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister.
● Also, the Supreme Court in R.C. Cooper versus Union of India, 1970 held that the President is merely a Constitutional Head and has to work only on the advice of the Council of Ministers.
● In essence **the President, as per the Constitution of India, doesn t have any power which** **he/she can discharge as per its discretion,** the advice of the Council of Ministers is paramount for the President to function. However, via the 44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978 the President was authorized to require the Council of Ministers to reconsider such advice. However, the advice tendered to him after reconsideration is binding on the President.
○ A bill passed by a State Legislature may also be reserved for the consideration of the President by the Governor of that State (under Article 200). The President enjoys veto power in relation to such a bill passed by a State Legislature. However, **he/she exercises this power only on** **the aid and advice of the Union Council of Ministers**. **So, point I is not correct.** ○ Article 143 empowers the President to consult the Supreme Court. The **President makes** **such a reference based on the advice of the Union council of ministers. It is not a** **discretionary power. So, point II is not correct.**
○ Situational discretion means those discretionary powers that are derived from the exigencies of a prevailing political situation. The President has certain situational discretion:
■ **Appointment of Prime Minister when no party has a clear majority in the Lok**
**Sabha** or when the Prime Minister in office dies suddenly and there is no obvious successor. **So, point III is correct.** ■ Dismissal of the council of ministers when it cannot prove the confidence of the Lok
Sabha. ■ Dissolution of the Lok Sabha if the council of ministers has lost its majority. **So, in only one of the above situations can the President of India use her discretion.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Prime Minister is considered to be the Keystone of the Cabinet Arch in India.
**Statement II:** The resignation of the Prime Minister automatically dissolves the Council of Ministers.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In India’s parliamentary government, **the President is the nominal executive while the Prime** **Minister (and the Council of Ministers) is the real executive.** Article 74 provides for a Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister to aid and advise the President in the exercise of his functions.
● According to Article 75, the Prime Minister shall be appointed by the President, and other ministers shall be appointed by the President upon the advice of the Prime Minister. **The Council of Ministers (or** **the Cabinet) is headed by the Prime Minister and its members exist on the will of the Prime** **Minister.** The President, acting upon the advice of the Prime Minister, distributes portfolios among the ministers.
● Dr. BR Ambedkar in the Constituent Assembly made a comment while discussing the office of the Prime Minister that **the Prime Minister is really the keystone of the arch of the Cabinet**. In the Westminster model of parliamentary governance (as is practiced in India), it is said that the **Prime** **Minister is central to the formation of the Cabinet, central to its life and central to its death**. The Government is the master of the country and the Prime Minister is the master of the government. **So, statement I is correct.**
● As the convention for the Westminster model goes, if the Cabinet discusses anything, it is the Prime Minister who decides what the collective view of the Cabinet is and if the Prime Minister wanted to take a certain step the Cabinet Minister concerned would either have to agree, argue it out in the Cabinet or resign.
○ Dr. BR Ambedkar speaking in the Constituent Assembly emphasized the **concept of collective** **responsibility** as an overarching principle of the parliamentary system and said that the Prime Minister is the only sanction through which collective responsibility could be enforced. That requires the enforcement of two principles — that no person be nominated to the Cabinet except on the advice of the Prime Minister and that no person be retained as minister if the Prime Minister wanted him to be dismissed.
● The Prime Minister stands at the head of the Council of Ministers, the other ministers cannot function when the Prime Minister resigns or dies. In other words, **the resignation or death of an incumbent** **Prime Minister automatically dissolves the Council of Ministers** and thereby generates a vacuum. The Prime Minister is thus the real maker and controller of the Council of Ministers. **So,** **statement II is correct.** **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Information Commission (CIC):
I. - It can consist of a maximum of 3 members.
II. 	 It can not take any action suo moto against an official.
III. 	 It submits its annual report to the President.
Which of the statements given above are not correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Central Information Commission (CIC)** is a **statutory body** under the provisions of the **Right to Information Act (2005)**. The CIC entertains complaints and appeals pertaining to offices, financial institutions, public sector undertakings, etc., under the Central Government and the Union Territories.
● The Commission consists of a **Chief Information Commissioner and not more than ten** **Information Commissioners.** Hence, it can consist of a maximum of 11 members. **So, statement** **I is not correct.**
● The Commission has certain powers and functions mentioned in sections 18, 19, 20 and 25 of the RTI Act, 2005. These broadly relate to adjudication in second appeal for giving information; direction for record keeping, **suo motu disclosures receiving and enquiring into a complaint** on inability to file RTI etc; imposition of penalties and Monitoring and Reporting including preparation of an Annual Report.
● **It can also initiate inquiries on its own (****suo motu****)** if there are reasonable grounds to do so. While conducting these inquiries, the Commission holds the authority of a civil court. **So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● The CIC is responsible for preparing an **annual report** that details the implementation of the provisions outlined in the Right to Information Act. The report is submitted to the **Central Government**, which is then presented before each House of Parliament. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● The CIC may recommend for disciplinary action against the Central Public Information Officer (CPIO) under the service rules if the Commission is of the opinion that the CPIO has, **without any reasonable** **cause and persistently failed** **to receive an application for information or has not furnished** **information** within the time specified under sub-section (1) of section 7 **or malafidely denied the** **request for information** or knowingly given incorrect, incomplete or misleading information or destroyed information which was the subject of the request or obstructed in any manner in furnishing the information. **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Composition, appointment and tenure of the CIC:**
● The **Chief Information Commissioner and the other Information** **Commissioners** are appointed by the **President of India** based on the recommendations of a **selection committee**.
● This committee is chaired by the **Prime Minister** and includes the **Leader of the** **Opposition** in the Lok Sabha and a **Union Cabinet Minister** nominated by the Prime Minister.
● The appointees must be **persons of eminence in public life**, with significant expertise in fields such as law, science and technology, social service, management, journalism, mass media, or administration and governance.
● They **cannot** be sitting Members of Parliament or members of any State or Union Territory legislature. Additionally, they must not hold any other **office of profit**, have any political affiliations, or engage in any business or professional activities.
● The Chief Information Commissioner, or Information Commissioners, as the case may be, shall hold office for a period of three years from the date on which he enters upon his office.
● Their salary, allowances, and other service conditions are set by the **Central** **Government**, but these cannot be altered to their disadvantage while they are in office, ensuring the security and independence of their positions. **Removal of the Chief Information Commissioner and the other Information** **Commissioners:**
● The **President** has the authority to **remove** the Chief Information Commissioner or any Information Commissioner under specific conditions, including:
○ if the individual is declared insolvent; ○ if convicted of an offense involving moral turpitude, as deemed by the President; ○ if the individual engages in paid employment outside the duties of their office; ○ if, in the President’s opinion, they are unfit to continue due to mental or physical infirmity; ○ if they acquire a financial or other interest that negatively affects their official duties.
● Additionally, removal can occur on grounds of **proven misbehavior or incapacity.** In such cases, the President must refer the matter to the **Supreme Court** for an inquiry.
● If the Supreme Court, after investigation, confirms the cause of removal and advises it, the President can then remove the concerned Commissioner.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With regard to the Union Executive, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. - The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Parliament.
II. 	 The Ministers are individually responsible to the President of India.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Parliamentary system of Government, that is prevalent in India, is based on a close relationship between the Executive and the Legislature. The Executive is accountable and responsible to the Legislature and stays in office as long as it enjoys the confidence of the Legislature.
● The fundamental principle underlying the working of the Parliamentary system of Government is this principle of Executive responsibility. **In Article 75 of the Constitution of India, the concept of** **both individual responsibility and collective responsibility are mentioned.**
● Article 75 (3) in the Constitution of India says that the **Council of Ministers shall be collectively** **responsible to the Lok Sabha**. This means that all the ministers own joint responsibility to the Lok Sabha for all their acts of omission and commission. **So, statement I is not correct.**
○ They work as a team and swim or sink together. When the Lok Sabha passes a no-confidence motion against the council of ministers, all the ministers have to resign including those ministers who are from the Rajya Sabha. It also means that ministers inside the government are required to put on a united face and officially support government policies and decisions.
● Article 75 also says that the **Ministers shall hold office during the pleasure of the President**. It means that the **Members of the Council of Ministers are individually responsible to the** **President of India**. This means that the President can remove a Minister even at a time when the Council of Ministers enjoys the confidence of the Lok Sabha. However, the President removes a minister only on the advice of the Prime Minister. Thus, in practice, the Ministers are responsible to the Prime Minister. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following situations:
I. - A member resigns from the party as he is elected to the post of Speaker
II. 	 A member voluntarily resigns from the party, if his party in opposition joins the treasury benches
III. 	 An independent member joins the ruling party
IV. 	 If a nominated member joins any political party within one month of his nomination
How many of the above situations will attract proceedings under the Tenth Schedule of the Constitution?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Tenth Schedule of the Constitution of India**, lays down the conditions under which legislators may **be disqualified on grounds of defection.** Enacted through the 52nd Amendment Act of 1985 and later strengthened by the 91st Amendment Act of 2003, it aims to curb political defections motivated by the lure of office or other incentives. However, the law also provides **certain exceptions and** **conditions** under which disqualification may not apply.
● **Grounds for Disqualification:** o **Voluntary Resignation from Party Membership:** A member can be disqualified if they
"voluntarily" give up their membership of the political party to which they belong regardless of whether the party moves from opposition to treasury. **So, point II is correct.** o **Defying Party Directives in Voting**: Disqualification can occur if a member votes or abstains
from voting in a legislative assembly in a manner that is contrary to any whip or direction issued by their party. o An **independent member** becomes disqualified **if he joins any political party** after the
election. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Exceptions:**
o If at **least two-thirds of the members** of a legislature party agree to merge with another party
or a new party formed by such a merger, they are **exempt from disqualification.** In such a scenario, the merged party or the new party is considered the member's original party for the purposes of the anti-defection law. o **Joining Another Party After Nomination:** If a member, after being nominated as a member
of the House, joins any political party within six months of assuming its seat, he/she is not liable for proceedings under the Xth schedule. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Election as Speaker** does not attract **disqualification**. It is because the post of Speaker is **constitutional and neutral** in nature, and resignation after election to this post does not imply defection. **So, point I is not correct.** **So, only two of the above situations will attract proceedings under the Tenth Schedule of the** **Constitution.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Joint Public Service Commission:
I. - An Act of the Parliament is required to establish the Joint Public Service Commission for two States.
II. 	 The members of the Joint Public Service Commission are appointed by the President.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Article 315(2) of the Constitution of India outlines the process for establishing **a Joint State Public** **Service Commission (JSPSC)** for two or more states. **JSPSC is a statutory and not a** **constitutional body.**
● A **JSPSC can** **be created by an Act of Parliament** on the request of the State Legislatures concerned. The Constitution states that if two or more States agree to have a single Public Service Commission, and each State's legislature (or each House in bicameral legislatures) passes a resolution, then Parliament can legislate to establish a JPSC. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The chairman and members of a JSPSC are **appointed by the President.** They hold office for a term of six years or until they attain the age of 62 years, whichever is earlier. They can be suspended or removed by the President. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The number of members of a JSPSC and their conditions of service are determined by the President. The JSPSC submits its annual report to the Governors of both States, who then present it to their respective State Legislatures. Its additional functions align with those performed by a State Public Service Commission. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Finance Commission:
I. - It functions as a quasi-judicial body.
II. 	 It recommends the measures needed to augment the Consolidated Fund of a State to supplement the resources of the Panchayats in the State.
III. 	 Its recommendations should be laid before the Parliament with an explanatory memorandum as to the action taken thereon.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Finance Commission** is a constitutional body under Article 280 of the Constitution of India. Its primary mandate is to make recommendations for the **distribution of central taxes between the** **Union and the States**, suggesting the allocation of grants-in-aid to States, proposing measures to strengthen state finances particularly for panchayats and municipalities and advising on any other matter referred to it by the President.
● The Commission has **quasi-judicial functions**. Despite not being a court, the Finance Commission s work involves **assessing financial needs, balancing the competing claims** of different states, and applying objective criteria in its recommendations. For Example, when recommending the horizontal distribution of tax revenues among states, it evaluates parameters such as population, income distance, area, forest cover, and fiscal discipline to ensure an equitable and transparent allocation. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● It is constituted every five years or earlier as deemed necessary by the President under Article 280 of the Constitution.
● It shall be the **duty of the Commission to make recommendations to the President** as to: o the **distribution between the Union and the States** of the net proceeds of taxes which are
to be, or may be, divided between them and the allocation between the States of the respective shares of such proceeds; o the principles which should govern the grants-in-aid of the revenues of the States out of the
Consolidated Fund of India;
▪ the measures needed to augment the **Consolidated Fund of a State to supplement** **the resources of the Panchayats in the State** on the basis of the recommendations made by the Finance Commission of the State. **So, statement II is correct.** ▪ the **measures needed to augment the Consolidated Fund of a State** to supplement the resources of the Municipalities in the State on the basis of the recommendations made by the Finance Commission of the State; ▪ any other matter referred to the Commission by the President in the interests of sound finance.
● **Article 280(2) of the Constitution of India** empowers Parliament to determine the qualifications for members of the Finance Commission, including the Chairman. While the Constitution itself doesn't specify detailed qualifications, it mandates that the President shall appoint a Chairman and other members as prescribed by law.
● **As per Article 281,** the President shall cause every recommendation made by the Finance Commission under the provisions of this Constitution together with an explanatory memorandum as to **the action** **taken thereon to be laid before each House of Parliament**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **The Finance Commission (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act, 1951,** was enacted for outlining the qualifications according to which the Chairman should have experience in public affairs and other members should be selected from among individuals who:
● are qualified to be appointed as judges of a High Court.
● possess special knowledge of the finances and accounts of government.
● have wide experience in financial matters and in administration.
● have special knowledge of economics.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is not correct?

options.a: The President can notify castes, races, or tribes to be considered as Scheduled Castes.
options.b: Only the President can notify any caste to be recognised as Socially and Educationally Backward classes for the purpose of State as well as Central list.
options.c: The State Government can provide for the sub-classification of Scheduled Castes.
options.d: The opinion of the National Commission Scheduled Tribes is not binding for the recognition of Scheduled-Tribes.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Part XVI of the Constitution of India provides for Special Provisions relating to Certain Classes, accordingly some castes or classes can be recognised as Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes or the Socially & Educationally Backward Classes (SEBCs)
● According to the Constitution under **Article 341**, **the President has the** **authority to specify** **castes as Scheduled Castes.** The President, through public notification, can specify which castes, races, or tribes are to be considered SCs in relation to a particular state or union territory, after consultation with the Governor. The Parliament can then make additions or deletions to the list of SCs specified by the President.
o It should be noted here that though the recognition of a Scheduled Castes is through a
Presidential order, **the amendment to the same can only be done by the Parliament.** o There is a separate list of Scheduled Castes for the Centre as well as States, but any change to
either of them can only be done by the President.
● **Article 342A,** as amended latest by the 105th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2021 specifies that, State or Union territory may, by law, prepare and maintain, for its own purposes, **a list of socially and** **educationally backward classes**, entries in which may be different from the Central List.
● **In a recent seven judge bench judgement the, the Supreme Court allowed  the State** **Governments to sub-classify the Scheduled Caste list for the targeted application of** **reservation policies.** Through this it reverted from its own earlier judgement delivered in the E.V. Chinnaiah (2004) case, where such exercise was explicitly barred by the court. Further, it said that the State Governments can sub-classify the SC list on its own as it doesn’t involve inclusion or exclusion from the list which may have required Parliamentary approval.
● The **National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (NCST)** was established to investigate and monitor matters relating to the safeguards provided for Scheduled Tribes. It also participates and **advises in the planning of socio-economic development** of Scheduled Tribes.
● While recognizing any class of persons as a Scheduled Tribe, though it is mandated to render the advice of the National Commission of Scheduled Tribes and the Registrar General of India, the recommendations of neither of them are binding on the President. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the recognition of minorities in India:
I. - The recognition of linguistic minorities in
India is done State wise, not nationally.
II. 	 At present, the religious minorities are
identified at the national level.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 30 of the Constitution of India** talks about only two kinds of minorities, that is, religious and linguistic minorities. The question of whether a particular community falls under the category of minority has to be decided on the basis of the demographic composition of the State, which means the proportion of their position in the population of different States and not the whole country.
● **Linguistic minorities** in India refer to groups or communities residing in any part of the country who have a distinct language or script of their own. Although the **Constitution does not provide a precise** **definition** of "linguistic minorities”,  it is well established that such groups **need not necessarily speak** **one of the 22 languages** listed in the Eighth Schedule.
● The Supreme Court in TMA Pai foundation versus the State of Karnataka, 2002 case stated that the **determination of Linguistic Minorities is to be done State wise**, not nationally. **So, statement** **I is correct**.
● The **Union Government set up the National Commission for Minorities (NCM) under the** **National Commission for Minorities Act**, 1992. Initially five religious communities, viz., Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, Buddhists and Zoroastrians (Parsis) were **notified as minority communities by** **the Union Government**. Further vide notification 2014, Jains were also notified as another minority community. **So, statement II is correct.**
o The Union Government constituted the National Commission for Minorities and the State
Government constituted State Minorities Commissions in their respective State Capitals. These organisations are set-up to **safeguard and protect the interests of minorities as provided** **in the Constitution of India** and laws enacted by the Parliament and the State Legislatures **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following Constitutional
Amendments gave Constitutional status to the
National Commission of Scheduled Castes?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **89th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003** established the **National Commission for** **Scheduled Tribes (NCST)** as a separate constitutional body by **bifurcating** the previously combined **National Commission for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes**. This amendment inserted **Article 338A** into the Constitution, exclusively dealing with the **NCST**.
● Before the amendment, a single commission was responsible for both Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs). The bifurcation created two distinct bodies: the **National Commission for** **Scheduled Castes (under Article 338)** and the **National Commission for Scheduled Tribes** **(under Article 338A)**.
● The Commission comprises a **Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, and three other Members**, all of whom are **appointed by the President of India**.
● The key functions and powers of the NCST include: o Investigating and monitoring the implementation of constitutional and legal safeguards for
Scheduled Tribes. o Inquiring into complaints of deprivation of rights and protections of STs. o Advising the Union and State governments on policy matters related to the socio-economic
development and welfare of Scheduled Tribes.
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
Original Constitution (26th January
**Article 338** - Special officer for the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes
Multi-Member Committee for welfare of SC and ST (non statutory)
Office of the Special officer for welfare of SCs and STs
National Commission of SC and ST (statutory)
Office of the Special officer for welfare of SCs and STs
65th Constitutional Amendment Act,
National Commission for SCs and STs (Constitutional body)
89th Constitutional Amendment Act,
National Commission for SC (Constitutional Body)
National Commission for ST (Constitutional Body)

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Union Public Service Commission
II. 	 Central Vigilance Commission
III. 	 National Commission for Scheduled Castes
IV. 	 National Human Rights Commission
How many of the above submit annual reports to
the President of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 323** of the Constitution of India pertains to reports of Public Service Commissions. According to article 323, the **Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) must present an annual report** **to the President**, who then causes it to be laid before each House of Parliament along with a memorandum explaining any non-acceptance of the Commission's advice. **So, point I is correct.**
o Each State Public Service Commission must present an annual report to the Governor, who then
causes it to be laid before the State Legislature along with a memorandum explaining any nonacceptance of the Commission's advice.
● As per the **Central Vigilance Commission Act, 2003**, the **Central Vigilance Commission** **(CVC)** is required to **submit an annual report to the President**. The President then causes it to be laid before both Houses of Parliament. The report includes observations on vigilance administration and anti-corruption efforts in the central government. **So, point II is correct.**
● According to **Article 338(5)(d)** of the Constitution, **the National Commission for Scheduled** **Castes (NCSC)** must submit an **annual report to the President**. The President then lays it before Parliament, along with a memorandum explaining the action taken on its recommendations and reasons for non-acceptance, if any. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)**, under the **Protection of Human Rights** **Act, 1993**, **submits its annual report to the Central Government**. The Central Government then lays it before Parliament along with an action-taken memorandum. **So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only three of the above bodies lay its annual report to the office of the President.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The term ‘office of profit’ is not defined
anywhere in the Constitution.
II. 	 The Parliament by law can exclude an office
from the purview of ‘office of profit’.
III. 	 The Presiding Officer of the House can
disqualify any member on account of holding
an office of profit.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under the provisions of **Article 102 (1) and Article 191 (1) of the Constitution of India,** a Member of Parliament or a Member of State Legislature is barred from holding any office of profit under the Central or State Government.
● Although mentioned explicitly in the Constitution, the term is **not defined anywhere** in it nor it is defined in the Representation of People’s Act, 1952. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Provisions of Articles 102 and 191 also protect a legislator occupying a government position if the office in question has been made immune to disqualification by law, that is, **the Parliament or the State** **legislature can declare which of the offices will not be deemed or classified as ‘office of** **profit’ for the purpose of Article 102 and Article 191. So, statement II is correct.**
● According to Article 103 of the Constitution, if any question arises as to whether a member of either House of Parliament has become subject to any of the disqualifications mentioned in Article 102(1), then **the question shall be referred to the President whose decision in the matter would be final**. Also, before giving any decision on the matter the President shall obtain the opinion of the Election Commission and shall act in accordance with such opinion. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
If a political party has to be recognized as a
National Party, then which one of the following
criteria must be fulfilled by it?

options.a:
It must be ‘recognized’ in two States as a
State party
options.b:
Its candidates should poll at least 3% of total
valid votes in four or more States in the last
Assembly elections
options.c:
It must secure 6% of the valid votes in two
States where it is not recognized as a State
party
options.d:
It must win at least 2% of the total seats in the
Lok Sabha from not less than three States

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order 1968 under the **Representation of People’s** **Act, 1951** governs the recognition of political parties at the National and the State level.
● For a political party to be recognized as a National Party, it should fulfil any of the following conditions: a) It is ‘recognized’ **in four or more states as a State party**; or b) If its candidates polled at **least 6% of total valid votes** in any **four or more States** in the last
Lok Sabha or Assembly elections and has **at least four MPs in the last Lok Sabha** polls; or **c)** If it **has won at least 2% of the total seats in the Lok Sabha** from not less than **three**
**States.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
For, a party to be recognized as **State party** must fulfil the following criteria:
● At least 6% vote-share in the last Assembly election and have at least 2 MLAs; or
● Have 6% vote-share in the last Lok Sabha elections from that state and at least one MP from that state; or
● At least 3% of the total number of seats or three seats, whichever is more, in the last Assembly elections; or
● At least one MP for every 25 members or any fraction allotted to the state in the Lok Sabha; or
● Have at least 8% of the total valid votes in the last Assembly election or Lok Sabha election from the state.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of
India acts as the "friend, philosopher, and guide"
to the:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG)** of India is an autonomous Constitutional authority tasked with auditing the financial accounts of the Government of India and its various agencies. Additionally, the CAG **evaluates the government's adherence to the Constitution and other** **relevant laws**.
● As an independent office the CAG is **responsible to the Parliament** only, to which it lays its report to, the office of the CAG is taken to as an agent of the Parliament which conducts audits of expenditure on its behalf.
● The CAG is a critical pillar of India’s democratic system of accountability. Its key contributions include: ○ **Agent of the Parliament:** The CAG **conducts audits on behalf of the Parliament** and is responsible to it. ○ **Link with Public Accounts Committee (PAC):** The CAG acts as a **“friend, philosopher,** **and guide” to the PAC of the Parliament**. The PAC examines the audit reports of the CAG,
which forms the basis of its scrutiny of government spending.
○ **Deterrent Against Mismanagement:** The CAG s audit reports highlight irregularities, wasteful expenditure and financial mismanagement, acting as a deterrent against corruption and inefficiency. By **bringing financial discrepancies to the notice of the legislature**, it ensures that the executive is held accountable **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Any person who is disqualified from the House on the charges of defection cannot be appointed as a Minister.
II. 	 Any person who has been disqualified on the basis of defection cannot contest re-election for the next five years.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Anti-Defection Law, introduced through the **Tenth Schedule** by the **52nd Constitutional** **Amendment Act, 1985**, seeks to curb political defections that undermine democratic stability. While the law **lays down grounds for disqualification**, subsequent constitutional amendments and judicial pronouncements have further clarified the implications of such disqualification.
● Any **person who is disqualified from the House** **on the charges of defection cannot be** **appointed as a minister in the same legislature.** This is a provision of the **91****st** **Constitutional** **Amendment Act, 2003** to the Constitution of India, which was enacted to strengthen the antidefection law.
● **Articles 75(1B) and 164(1B)** (for Union and State respectively), which state that a disqualified member **cannot become a minister from the date of disqualification until the end of the term** **of the legislature**. Similarly, **Article 361B** bars them from holding any **remunerative political post** during this period. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Supreme Court in **Shrimanth Balasaheb Patil vs Hon'ble Speaker Karnataka Legislative** **Assembly** has clarified that **disqualification under the Tenth Schedule does not create a ban on** **contesting elections**. This means a member disqualified for defection can **recontest and return** to the legislature through a fresh mandate. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Subject:** Polity | Anti-Defection Law **Tags:** Factual

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Misbehavior
II. 	 Incapacity
III. 	 Insolvency
How many of the above are constitutional grounds for the impeachment of the President of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 61 of the Constitution of India** describes the conditions and procedure for the removal of the incumbent President of India from its office. As per the Article, **the President can be removed** **from office by a process of impeachment for ‘violation of the Constitution’**. However, the Constitution does not define the meaning of the phrase ‘violation of the constitution’.
● Hence, **the only constitutional ground for the removal of the President is the 'violation of** **the constitution’. So, points I, II and III are not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Procedure for the impeachment of the President of India:**
● The President can be impeached for violation of the Constitution.
● The impeachment charges can be initiated by either House of Parliament.
● These charges should be signed by one-fourth members of the House (that framed the charges).
● A 14 days’ notice should be given to the President before such charges are initiated.
● When such a resolution has been passed by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the total membership of the House, it is referred to another House.
● The other House shall investigate the charge or cause the charge to be investigated. The President shall have the right to appear and to be represented at such an investigation.
● If the other House also passes the resolution by a two-third majority, declaring that the charge preferred against the President has been sustained. Then such resolution shall have the effect of removing the President from his office as from the date on which the resolution is so passed.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Delimitation Commission:
I. - It is an executive body appointed by the Election Commission of India.
II. 	 Its orders cannot be challenged in any court on any ground.
III. 	 It was last constituted in 2002 under the Delimitation Act, 2002.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Delimitation is the act of redrawing the boundaries of Lok Sabha and State Assembly constituencies to represent changes in population. **Delimitation is constitutionally mandated under Articles 82** **and 170 of the Indian Constitution.**
● Delimitation is carried out by an **independent statutory body** called the Delimitation Commission. It is appointed for the purpose of drawing up the boundaries of constituencies all over the country. It also fixes and decides on the quota of constituencies to be reserved in each State for SC or ST.
● The Delimitation Commission is **appointed by the President of India** and works in collaboration with the Election Commission of India. It is composed of the following: a retired judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court, the Chief Election Commissioner of India and the respective State Election Commissioners. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The Delimitation Commission in India is a high power body **whose orders have the force of law.** These orders come into force on a date to be specified by the President of India on this behalf. The copies of its orders are laid before the House of the People and the State Legislative Assembly concerned, but no modifications are permissible therein by them. Once published, the Commission’s orders are final and, as per the Delimitation Commission Act 1952 and Article 329A of the Constitution, has the “full force of law and shall not be called into question in any court”.
● The Supreme Court in its judgement in **Kishorchandra Chhanganlal Rathod vs. Union of India**, 2024 held that the **constitutional courts can review** the orders of the delimitation commission and can also grant appropriate remedy if an order is manifestly arbitrary and irreconcilable to constitutional values. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Delimitation Commission is set up after the passage of a Delimitation Act by parliament** **from time to time.** The responsibilities of the commissions are individually prescribed by each Act. **Four commissions have been established till date in 1952, 1962, 1972 and 2002. So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - The President of India
II. 	 Attorney General for India
III. 	 The nominated members of the Rajya Sabha
IV.  	The elected members of the Lok Sabha
How many of the above vote on the resolution for
the removal of the Vice-President of India from
office?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 67 of the Constitution of India** makes provision regarding tenure and removal of VicePresident of India. The Vice-President shall hold office for a term of five years from the date on which he enters upon his office. However, he may be removed before completion of his tenure.
● The Vice-President may be removed from his office **by a resolution of the Council of States (Rajya** **Sabha).** Such a resolution must be **passed by a majority of all the then members of the Rajya** **Sabha.** Thus, **both nominated and elected members of Rajya Sabha participate in the** **removal process. So, point III is correct.**
○ A resolution for the removal of Vice-President of India is to be moved only after giving at least fourteen days' prior notice.
● Such a resolution then needs to be **agreed to by the House of the People (Lok Sabha)**. That means it is to be passed by elected members of Lok Sabha by a simple majority. **So, point IV is correct.**
● The **Attorney General of India does not participate in the removal of the Vice President;** the Parliament, specifically the Rajya Sabha and Lok Sabha, carries out this process. The Attorney General is a legal advisor to the government and can participate in parliamentary proceedings, but does not have the right to vote, and the removal of the Vice President is a parliamentary decision, not a legal advisory one.  **So, point II is not correct.**
● The resolution for the removal of Vice-President just like that of the **Speaker of Lok Sabha**, post its passage from both the Houses is **not sent to the President for assent, the President is just** **communicated on the same**. **So, point I is not correct.** **So, only two of the above participate in the process for removal of the Vice-President of India** **from office.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the election of the Members
of the Rajya Sabha, consider the following
statements:
I. - The election is conducted on the basis of
First Past the Post (FPTP) system.
II. 	 Members from Union Territories are
indirectly elected by an electoral college.
III. 	 The Rajya Sabha members are elected
through an open ballot system.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Rajya Sabha is the Upper House of the Indian Parliament**. Rajya Sabha consists of the representatives of the States and the Union Territories and persons nominated by the President of India. The maximum strength of the Rajya Sabha is fixed at 250. At present, the Rajya Sabha has 245 members. Of these, 229 members represent the States, 4 members represent the Union Territories and 12 members are nominated by the President.
● The representatives of States in the Rajya Sabha are elected by the elected members of State Legislative Assemblies. The election is held in accordance **with the system of proportional representation by** **means of the single transferable vote**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
○ The representatives of each Union Territory in the Rajya Sabha are indirectly elected by members of an electoral college specially constituted for the purpose. This election is also held in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ **The system of territorial representation (First-past-the-post system) is adopted for** **the election of members to the Lok Sabha.**
● **The Rajya Sabha elections are presently conducted by open ballot,** prior to 2001 these elections were conducted via a secret ballot, later the Central Government passed an amendment to facilitate open ballot voting, which was subsequently upheld by the Supreme Court in Kuldip Nayar versus the Union of India case. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following would preside over
the Joint Sitting of the Parliament in case both
Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha
are absent?

options.b: Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A joint sitting of both Houses is convened by the President under **Article 108** **of the Constitution of** **India**. It is convened to resolve the deadlock between the two Houses over a bill.
● A disagreement between the two Houses may arise when a Bill passed by one House is rejected by the other House; or the Houses have finally disagreed as to the amendments to be made in the Bill; or more than six months have elapsed from the date of receipt of the Bill by the other House without the Bill being passed by it.
● **The Speaker of Lok Sabha presides** over a joint sitting of the two Houses and the **Deputy** **Speaker, in his absence.** If the Deputy Speaker is also absent from a joint sitting, the **Deputy** **Chairman of Rajya Sabha** presides. If he is also absent, such other person as may be determined by the members present at the joint sitting, presides over the meeting.
○ The Chairman of Rajya Sabha does not preside over a joint sitting as he is not a member of either House of Parliament. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with regard to the Leader of the Opposition in Lok Sabha?
I. - It is constitutionally mandatory to appoint the Leader of the Opposition.
II. 	 He is ranked equal to the Union Cabinet Ministers in the Order of Precedence.
III. 	 He acts as the Chief Whip for all the opposition parties.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In each House of the Parliament, there are provisions for the Leader of the Opposition (LoP). The LoP is a Member of Parliament who is in charge of the Opposition and the parliamentary chairperson of the largest opposition party. The role of LoP is to ensure accountability of the government to the Legislature and to the public.
● The position of Leader of Opposition was officially described in **The Salary and Allowances of** **Leaders of Opposition in Parliament Act, 1977**. The Act describes the Leader of Opposition as a “member of the Council of States or the House of the People, as the case may be, who is, for the time being, the Leader in that House of the party in opposition to the Government having the greatest numerical strength and recognised as such by the Chairman of the Council of States or the Speaker of the House of the People”. **The Constitution neither mentions about the post of LoP, nor puts** **an obligation on the House to create or appoint to the office. So, statement I is not correct.**
● The LoPs are also **entitled to the salary, allowances and other facilities equivalent to that of** **a Cabinet Minister.** They are ranked equivalent to the Union Cabinet Ministers in the Order of Precedence. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The main duty of the Leader of Opposition is to serve as the voice of the opposition in the House. An official booklet on Parliament published in 2012 says the Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha “is considered as a shadow Prime Minister with a shadow Cabinet, ready to take over the administration if the Government resigns or is defeated on the floor of the House”.
● Though LoP is considered as a joint leader of the opposition benches, which may have members from various Political Parties, **he is not an authority over the members of the opposition benches in** **the official capacity**. Appointing whips is an internal matter of the Parties, and **each party having** **representation in the House can appoint any of its members as a whip**. **So, statement III is** **not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Why for the LoP position there is a minimum requirement for attaining minimum** **10% of the Seats in the House?**
● As per the **The Salary and Allowances of Leaders of Opposition in Parliament** **Act, 1977** it is upon the Speaker/Chairperson of the House to recognize a LoP, the only precondition being that the person should be leader of the largest opposition party in the House. As per the act, there is no rule stating that the appointment of LoP only if the largest opposition party gets at least 10% of the seats in the House.
● This precondition is derived from the **Mavlankar rule** given by GV Mavlankar, the first speaker of the Lok Sabha. According to this rule, a party must have at least sufficient numbers in the House to ensure a quorum, that is 10% of the total seats, in order to be designated as a Parliamentary Party.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following functionaries:
I. - Chairperson of the National Council for Transgender Persons
II. 	 Chairperson of the National Commission for Protection of Child Rights
III. 	 Chairperson of the National Commission for Safai Karamcharis
IV.	 Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities
How many of the above serve as the ex-officio Members of the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)** is an independent **multi-member statutory** **body**, first established in the year 1993, under the **Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993**. The commission serves as a guardian of human rights in India, overseeing the rights associated with life, liberty, equality, and the dignity of individuals.
● The NHRC consists of a **chairperson** and **five full time members and seven** **ex-officio** **Members**, each bringing a wealth of experience and expertise to the commission.
● The composition of the commission is as follows: ○ **Full-Time Members:** ■ **Chairperson:** He/she should be either a retired Chief Justice of India or a judge of the
Supreme Court. ■ **Members:** These include a sitting or retired judge of the Supreme Court, a sitting or
retired Chief Justice of a High Court, and three individuals **(with at least one being a** **woman)** who possess knowledge or practical experience in human rights. ○ **Seven Ex-officio Members:** In addition to these full-time members, the commission includes seven ex-officio members, comprising the **chairpersons of various national commissions**, including:
■ The National Commission for Minorities ■ The National Commission for Scheduled Castes (SCs) ■ The National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (STs) ■ The National Commission for Women ■ The National Commission for Backward Classes (BCs) ■ **The National Commission for Protection of Child Rights (NCPCR). So, point**
**II is correct.** ■ **The Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (CCPwD). So, point IV**
**is correct.**
● The **National Council for Transgender Persons (NCTP)** is a **statutory body** constituted under the **Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Act 2019** to safeguard the rights of transgender persons in India.
○ The NCTP is responsible for monitoring and evaluating the **implementation of the provisions** **of the Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Act, 2019** and making recommendations to the government on measures to promote and protect the rights of transgender persons.
● The **National Commission for Safai Karamcharis (NCSK)** was first set up in 1994 under the **NCSK Act, 1993.**
○ It was functioning as a statutory body till 2004, when the law lapsed. ○ Since then, the commission has been acting as a **non-statutory body** under the Social Justice Ministry with its tenure being extended from time to time. ○ It was established as an institution to investigate the conditions of Safai Karamcharis (waste collectors) in India and **make recommendations to the Government.**
● The Chairpersons of the NCTP and the NCSK are **not ex officio members** of the NHRC. **So, point** **I and point III are not correct.** **So, only two of the above functionaries serve as the ex-officio Members of the National Human** **Rights Commission (NHRC).** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - All the members of All India Services work at the pleasure of the President.
II. 	 Any member of the All India Services cannot be removed from service by any authority lower than the one who appoints them.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Part XIV of the Constitution of India** contains provisions for regulating the employment of persons
to the public services under the Union and the States**. Article 312** of the Constitution explicitly provides
for the creation of these services, establishing them as shared administrative resources between the
Union and State governments, while **Article 311** provides for the terms of service of these officers.
● The **Doctrine of Pleasure** is an instrument of public policy which states that any servant to the
sovereign (herein the President of India) serves at the pleasure of the President, this implies that the
servant can be removed at will by the President and the former cannot object to his/her dismissal from
service.
● **Article 310** of the Constitution states that all the civil servants of the Union (members of the Defence Services, Civil Services, All-India Services or persons holding military posts or civil posts under the Centre/State) **hold office at the pleasure of the President**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● But the doctrine in India is not unrestricted and is subject to provisions of the Constitution as mentioned elsewhere in the statute.
● According to **Article 311** of the Constitution**, a member of an All India Service cannot be** **removed from their office by an authority that is lower in rank than the one that appointed** **them**. This provision protects civil servants from arbitrary dismissal and ensures they are given a fair hearing before any disciplinary action is taken. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct
with regard to Money Bills in India?
I. - It can be introduced only by a Minister and
not by any other member of the Lok Sabha.
II. 	 It can neither be rejected nor amended in the
Rajya Sabha.
III. 	 The Speaker has the final authority in
deciding whether a Bill is a Money Bill or
not.
IV. 	 The President cannot withhold her assent to a
Money Bill.
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **T**he Constitution of India provides that a bill will be deemed as Money Bill if it only deals with any or all of the matters mentioned in Article 110. Money bills **can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha** and only with the recommendation of the President. Money bills are considered government bills and **can** **only be introduced by a minister. So, statement I is correct.**
● The Rajya Sabha **cannot initiate, reject, or amend money bills**. The Constitution lays down a special procedure for the passing of money bills in the Parliament. A money bill can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha. After a money bill is passed by the Lok Sabha, it is transmitted to the Rajya Sabha for its consideration. The Rajya Sabha has restricted powers with regard to a money bill. **It cannot reject or** **amend a money bill**. It can only make the recommendations. It must return the bill to the Lok Sabha within 14 days, whether with or without recommendations. The Lok Sabha can either accept or reject all or any of the recommendations of the Rajya Sabha**. So, statement II is correct.**
● If any question arises whether a Bill is a Money Bill or not, **the decision of the Speaker of the House** **of the People thereon shall be final.** However, the Supreme Court in the Aadhar judgement of 2018 held that the Speaker’s decision on whether a Bill is a money Bill or not, despite being “final” according to the Constitution, can still be subject to judicial review. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The President **may either give or withhold his assent to a Money Bill.** A Money Bill can not be returned to the House by the President for reconsideration.  **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which of the following provisions was/were
added by the 91st Constitutional Amendment Act
of 2003?
I. - No disqualification proceedings if one third
members of a party split to form a separate
group.
II. 	 The total number of Ministers at the Union
level shall not exceed 15 percent of the
strength of the Lok Sabha.
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **91****st** **Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003** was enacted to strengthen the provisions of the **Anti-Defection Law** and to curb the proliferation of ministerial posts. It aimed at **limiting the size** **of Council of Ministers** and debar people disqualified due to defection from holding public offices.
● **Some of the significant provisions of the 91****st** **amendment act include:** o Initially, as per the provision of the Tenth Schedule, there **was no disqualification of**
**members if one third members** of a party split to form a separate group. **The 91****st** **amendment deleted this provision pertaining to exemption from disqualification on** **the ground of splits.** However, if two-thirds of a legislative party agree to merge with another party, it's considered a valid merger, and the members involved are not subject to disqualification. **So, statement I is not correct.**
o The total number of ministers, including the Prime Minister, in the Central Council of
Ministers **shall not exceed 15%** of the **total strength of the Lok Sabha**. **So, statement II** **is correct.** o A member of either House of Parliament affiliated with any political party, who is disqualified
based on defection, is also **ineligible for appointment as a minister**. o The total number of ministers, including the Chief Minister, in the Council of Ministers in a
state **shall not exceed 15%** of the total strength of the Legislative Assembly of that state. However, the number of ministers, including the Chief Minister, in a state shall not be less than
12. o A member of either House of Parliament or either House of State Legislature associated with
any political party, facing disqualification on the grounds of defection, is also barred from holding any remunerative political position. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Election Commission of India(ECI):
I. - The Representation of People’s Act 1951
explicitly authorises the ECI to  de-register a
political party.
II. 	 Scheduling of the elections to both Central
and State Legislatures is an exclusive
prerogative of the ECI.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Election Commission of India (ECI)** is a **permanent and independent constitutional** **body**, operating both at the **Central and State levels**. The Constitution empowers the ECI with the **superintendence, direction, and control** of elections to Parliament and State Legislatures.
● The ECI has the power to **register political parties** under **Section 29A** of the **RPA, 1951**. However, **it does not have the power to de-register** a political party. The Supreme Court has held that such power is not explicitly conferred on the ECI. De-registration can only happen under very limited circumstances (e.g., a party ceases to exist, or voluntarily seeks de-registration). **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
● The scheduling of elections to the **Lok Sabha, State Legislative Assemblies, and Rajya Sabha is** **an exclusive prerogative of the Election Commission of India (ECI)** under Article 324 of the Constitution. This includes announcing the election calendar, determining polling dates, and enforcing the Model Code of Conduct (MCC) to ensure free and fair elections. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following Urban Local Bodies
is not established by the State Government?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The term **Urban Local Government** in India signifies the governance of an urban area by the people through their elected representatives. The jurisdiction of an urban local government is limited to a specific urban area which is demarcated for this purpose. The governance for these areas is vested in Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) established by appropriate authority:
● **Port Trusts:** These are established in port areas to manage and protect ports and provide civic amenities. **They are created by an Act of the Parliament.**
● **Municipal Corporation:** These are ULBs created for administration of big cities. They are established in the States **by an act of the concerned State legislature**.
● **Notified Area Committee:** It is created for the administration of two types of areas – a fast developing town due to industrialization, and a town which does not yet fulfil all the conditions necessary for the constitution of municipality, but which is otherwise considered important by the State Government. **It is established via a notification by the State Government.**
● **Town Area Committee:** It is generally set up for the administration of a small town. It is a semimunicipal authority and is entrusted with a limited number of civic functions. **It is created by a** **separate act of a State Legislature**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Union Territories (UTs):
I. - The Lieutenant Governor/Administrator of
an UT has the same status as the Head of
State like a Governor.
II. 	 The President can establish a High Court for
a UT or put it under jurisdiction of the High
Court of the adjacent State.
III. The President can make provisions for the
peace, progress, and good government of
specific Union territories.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following States:
I. - Assam
II. 	 Manipur
III. 	 Meghalaya
IV. 	 Nagaland
V. 	 Tripura In how many of the above states autonomous districts can be established under the Sixth Schedule of the Constitution?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Constitution, under **Sixth Schedule**, contains special provisions for the administration of tribal
areas in the four north eastern states of **Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram. So, only**
**points I, III and V are correct.**
● The various features of administration contained in the Sixth Schedule are as follows:
o The tribal areas in the four states of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram have been
constituted as **autonomous districts**. But, they do not fall outside the executive authority of
the state concerned.
o **The governor is empowered to organise and re-organise the autonomous districts.**
