Thus, he can increase or decrease their areas or change their names or define their boundaries and so on. o If there are different tribes in an autonomous district, the governor can divide the district into
several autonomous regions. o Each autonomous district has a district council consisting of 30 members, of whom four are
nominated by the governor and the remaining 26 are elected on the basis of adult franchise. The elected members hold office for a term of five years (unless the council is dissolved earlier) and nominated members hold office during the pleasure of the governor. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The Rajya Sabha can initiate the process of creating an All India Service by a resolution supported by two-third majority of members present and voting.
II. 	 The creation of the All India Judicial Service does not require a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368.
III. 	 The Constitution empowers the Parliament to regulate recruitment and conditions of service of persons appointed to All India Services.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 312** of the Constitution of India **empowers the Rajya Sabha (Council of States) to** **initiate the process of creating a new All India Services by passing a resolution supported** **by not less than two-thirds of members present and voting.** After the resolution is passed by the Rajya Sabha it is then sent to the Lok Sabha, where it needs to be passed by a simple majority. This provision acknowledges that while administration is primarily a state subject under India’s federal structure, certain services need to maintain national standards and uniformity. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● Article 312  further states that **any such resolution to create a new All India Service will not be** **restricted by the provisions as mentioned in the Chapter VI of Part VI or Part XI of the** **Constitution.**For All India Judicial Services (AIJS)**,** the **42****nd** **Amendment Act, 1976, which** **provided for creation of All India Judicial Services**, specifically mentioned that for the creation of such a service if any provision under Part VI of the constitution needed to be amended, it **will not be** **taken to as an amendment under Article 368** of the constitution. The Parliament has the full authority to do the same via normal legislative process and any change to the text of constitution whilst doing so, will not be deemed as a constitutional amendment. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Article 312 of the Constitution confers power on **Parliament to regulate recruitment and** **conditions of service of persons appointed to All India Services**. **So, statement III is correct.**
○ However, the All India Services Act, 1951 provides that the rules for the regulation of recruitment and the conditions of services of members of the All India Services shall be made by the Union Government after consultation with the State Governments (vide Section 3). **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText: Which one of the following presents, on behalf of the President, the Presidential Reference made under Article 143 of the Constitution to the Supreme Court of India?

options.a: The Union Minister of Law and Justice

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 143 of the Constitution of India** authorizes the president to seek the opinion of the Supreme Court in the two categories of matters:
○ On any question of **law or fact of public importance** which has arisen or which is likely to arise. In this case, the Supreme Court **may tender or may refuse to tender its opinion** to the president. ○ On any **dispute arising out of any pre-constitution treaty**, agreement, covenant, engagement, sanad or other similar instruments. In this case, the Supreme Court ‘**must’ tender** **its opinion** to the president.
● In both the cases, the opinion expressed by the Supreme Court is **only advisory and not a judicial** **pronouncement**. Hence, it is **not binding on the president**; he may follow or may not follow the opinion. However, it facilitates the government to have an authoritative legal opinion on a matter to be decided by it.
● The **Attorney General of India** represents the Government of India and presents the Presidential Reference made under Article 143 of the Constitution to the Supreme Court of India on behalf of the President.
● So far, the President has made **sixteen references** to the Supreme Court under its advisory jurisdiction (also known as consultative jurisdiction). **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● The advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under Article 143 is a relic of the **Government of India Act, 1935.** It vested the **Governor-General** with discretionary power to refer any question of law of public importance to the federal court for its opinion.
● A similar provision is available in the **Canadian constitution**. This mechanism allows the Supreme Court of Canada to offer opinions on legal questions referred to it by the federal or provincial governments.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Union Public Service Commission (UPSC):
I. - It provides advisory opinions to the Central Government in disciplinary or vigilance matters of its employees.
II. 	 It decides the quantum and allocation of reserved seats for various posts.
III. 	 Its jurisdiction can be extended by the President through a notification.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Union Public Service Commission (UPSC)** serves as the central recruiting agency of India and functions as an independent constitutional body. It operates autonomously to uphold transparency, fairness, and efficiency in the recruitment process.
● The UPSC **offers advisory recommendations to the government**, but these are not binding. The government's decisions based on UPSC’s advice are open to parliamentary scrutiny. It must also be noted here, that in disciplinary or vigilance matters, the government receives advisory opinions from both UPSC and CVC. **So, statement I is correct.**
o **HS Bedi versus. Govt. of Patiala and East Punjab States Union, 1953** the Court held that
the role of UPSC is directory not mandatory.
● The government is **constitutionally required to consult the UPSC** on various matters related to personnel management, including recruitment, promotions, transfers, and disciplinary actions. However, the Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) in India **does not have jurisdiction over certain** **matters and is not consulted** in the following cases:
o Reservation of appointments or posts for backward classes o Claims of Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in appointments o Selection for posts such as chairpersons or members of commissions and tribunals, key diplomatic
roles, and most Group C and Group D services, o Temporary or officiating appointments lasting less than one year.
● **Reservation policy** **(including quantum and allocation of reserved seats for SCs, STs, OBCs,** **EWS, etc.) is as decided by the** **appropriate authority**. The UPSC only conducts exams and recruitment as per the reservation policy laid down by the Government. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● Under Article 321 of the Constitution**, the Parliament can extend the functions (i.e.,** **jurisdiction) of the UPSC in respect of the services of the Union or the State**, through regulation. The Parliament also has the authority to exclude specific posts, services, and matters from UPSC’s purview. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
A division is a type of voting which records how each Member of Parliament (MP) voted on a motion. With reference to division of votes, consider the following statements:
I. - Any member of the Parliament can demand a division of votes on a motion.
II. 	 It is a necessary precondition for the passage of Constitutional Amendment Acts.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● All decisions in Parliament are taken by voting by Members of Parliament (MPs). **Voice voting** is the preferred method of decision making by the Parliament. Members in favour of a decision call out “Ayes” and those opposed say “Noes”. The Speaker then takes a call on which voices were louder and conveys the decision of the House.
● **The rules of procedure of Lok Sabha do not mandate recording of votes of MPs for every**
**decision taken.** Voice voting does not reveal the individual positions taken by MPs.
● **A division is a type of voting which records how each MP voted on a motion**. If a member is
not happy with a voice vote, it can be challenged and a division can be asked for. **MPs also have the**
**right to ask for the vote of every MP to be recorded.** This is called a division. **So, statement I**
**is correct.**
● Recording of votes is also mandated **when there is a constitutional requirement** for a special
majority of Parliament (for example a constitutional amendment), or after a no-confidence motion. **So,**
**statement II is correct.**
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC):
I. - The 100th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2018, which inserted Article 338B, gave constitutional recognition to the NCBC.
II. 	 The President has the authority to determine the composition of the NCBC.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC)** was initially a statutory body set up under the National Commission for Backward Classes Act, 1993. However, it was later given constitutional status by the **102****nd** **Constitutional Amendment Act, 2018,** which inserted Article 338B. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) plays a vital role in promoting **equitable** **access to employment, education**, and other socio-economic opportunities for the backward classes. It aims to protect and promote the rights and interests of socially and educationally backward classes in India.
● While Article 338B of the Constitution of India establishes the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC), it **doesn't specify the exact number of members**. Instead, it grants the President the authority to determine the composition, including the Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, and other members, through rules. **So, statement II is correct.**
o The **President also determines** their terms of service and tenure through prescribed rules.
Additionally, the Commission has the authority to **formulate its own procedures** for conducting its functions. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI):
I. - It cannot take over any case registered in a State without the concurrence of the latter.
II. 	 It must seek the Centre’s permission before registering a corruption case against any government servant.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)** is an investigative agency under the overall purview of the Central Government It derives its authority from the Delhi Special Police Establishment (DSPE) Act, 1946. The provisions of this Act primarily apply **to the areas under the direct jurisdiction of the** **Central Government including the Union Territories.** The jurisdiction of the bureau can be extended to the States only with their consent.
● As per Section 6 of the Delhi Special Police Establishment (DSPE) Act, 1946, the **CBI needs the** **consent from the respective State Government for conducting investigation in its** **jurisdiction.** Some State governments have granted a general consent to CBI for the investigation of specified class of offences against specified categories of persons enabling CBI to register and investigate those specified matters.  Also, the Central Government can authorize the CBI to investigate such crime in a State, but only with the consent of the concerned State Government.
● In addition to it, the Supreme Court and the High Courts, can order CBI to investigate such a crime anywhere in the country without the consent of the State Government. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● In 2018, the government pushed through Parliament amendments to Section 17A of the Act making it mandatory for the CBI to **seek the Centre’s permission** before registering a case of corruption against a government servant. **In January 2025, the Supreme Court ruled that the CBI does** **need permission to register FIR against Centre's officers posted in a different jurisdiction** **in a state**. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● In 2024, the Karnataka Government withdrew general consent to the CBI, becoming the eighth state to do so. Other states include Punjab, Jharkhand, Kerala, West Bengal, Telangana, Meghalaya, and Tamil Nadu. Except for Meghalaya, all these states are ruled by parties in Opposition at the Centre.
● Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh, Mizoram and Chhattisgarh too had earlier withdrawn consent but restored the same following government change.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Changing the allocation of Lok Sabha seats among the States.
II. 	 Presentation of the Annual Financial Statement by a Minister other than the Finance Minister
III. 	 Abolishing the post of the Deputy Speaker of House of the People
IV. 	 Changing the nomenclature of the Council of States in Parliamentary proceedings
How many of the above will require an amendment to the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● While the seats in the Rajya Sabha are distributed amongst the States via the Fourth Schedule of the Constitution Article 82 provides that upon the completion of each census, **the allocation of seats in** **the House of the People to the States** and the division of each State into territorial constituencies **shall be readjusted by such authority and in such manner as Parliament may by law** **determine. Thus,** the **Parliament by law enacts a Delimitation Act** after every census. Thus, the readjustment of seats requires ordinary legislation, and not Constitutional Amendment. **So, point I is** **not correct.**
● Article 112 says that the President shall in respect of every financial year cause to be laid before both the Houses of Parliament the “annual financial statement”. By convention, the **Union** **Finance Minister** **presents the “annual financial statement” or budget on behalf of the Prime Minister.** The Prime Minister can allow any other Minister to present the Budget. This does not require a Constitutional Amendment. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Article 93 of Indian Constitution provides for the Speaker and Deputy Speaker** of the House of the People. This post is mandated under Article 93 of the Constitution of India. Article 93 clearly states: “The House of the People shall choose two members of the House to be respectively Speaker and Deputy Speaker.” Thus, abolishing this post would require amendment to Article 93.  **So, point III** **is correct.**
● **The Constitution of India refers to the Houses of Parliament as the ‘Council of States’ and** **the ‘House of People’**. In 1954 the Hindi names ‘Rajya Sabha’ and ‘Lok Sabha’ were adopted by the respective Houses. Hence, changing this nomenclature doesn’t require any amendment to the Constitution. **So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only one of the above will require an amendment to the Constitution of India.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the office of Lokpal:
I. - Its jurisdiction extends to the Council of Ministers including the Prime Minister.
II. 	 Institutions partly financed by the Government of India come under its purview.
III.	 It can investigate Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) receiving foreign aid.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Lokpal is an independent statutory body** established under Section 3 of the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013.  In terms of Section 48 of the said Act, the Lokpal is required to present annually to the President a report on the work done by it, which is caused to be laid in both the Houses of the Parliament.
● It has jurisdiction to inquire into allegations of corruption against **anyone who is or has been the** **Prime Minister of India**, **a Minister in the Union Government,** or a Member of Parliament, as well as officials of the Union Government under Groups A, B, C, and D. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ **Exceptions to Prime Minister:** Lokpal cannot inquire into allegations against the PM relating to international relations, external and internal security, public order, atomic energy, and space. Also, complaints against the PM are not to be probed unless the full Lokpal bench considers the initiation of the inquiry and at least 2/3rds of the members approve it.
● Institutions which are “financed” fully or partly by the Government come under the jurisdiction of Lokpal, **So, statement II is  correct.**
● The **jurisdiction of Lokpal extends to the Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs)** **receiving foreign aid as well**, the Lokpal can receive complaints and initiate inquiry against the NGOs receiving more than 10 Lakh rupees a year as foreign aid. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following functionaries:
I. - Union Home Minister
II. 	 Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha
III. 	 Speaker of Lok Sabha
IV. 	 Chief Justice of India
How many of the above are part of the selection committees for appointments to the National Human Rights Commission and the Lokpal?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**National Human Rights Commission (NHRC):**
● The NHRC is an independent **multi-member statutory body**, established on October 12, 1993, under the **Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993**.
● The commission serves as a guardian of human rights in India, overseeing the rights associated with life, liberty, equality, and the dignity of individuals.
● The appointment of the chairperson and members of the NHRC is carried out by the **President of** **India** based on the recommendations of a **six-member committee**. This committee is headed by the **Prime Minister** and includes the following members:
○ **Speaker of the Lok Sabha**, ○ Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, ○ **Leaders of the Opposition** in both Houses of Parliament, ○ Union Home Minister.
● The chairperson and members of the NHRC serve for a **term of three years** or until they reach the age of **70 years**, whichever is earlier. They are **eligible for reappointment** following the completion of their term. However, once their tenure concludes, neither the chairperson nor the members are permitted to seek further employment with the Central or State governments. **Lokpal:**
● The Lokpal is a **statutory body** established under the **Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act 2013**.
● The mandate of the Lokpal is to **inquire into allegations of corruption against** certain **public** **functionaries** and for related matters. The Lokpal consists of a **chairperson and eight Members,** out of whom **four are Judicial Members.**
● The Chairperson and the Members are **appointed by the President** of India on the **recommendation of a selection committee composed of** the **Prime Minister** as the Chairperson, the **Speaker of Lok Sabha,** the **Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha,** the **Chief** **Justice of India** or a **Judge nominated by him**/her, and **one eminent jurist.**
**Member** **Committee** **for** **selection of NHRC** **members**
**Lokpal** **Selection** **Committee**
**Remarks**
**Union** **Home** **Minister**
✅ Yes ❌ No **So, point I is not** **correct.**
**Leader of** **Oppositio** **n in Lok** **Sabha**
✅ Yes ✅ Yes **So,** **point**
**II** **is** **correct.**
**Speaker of** **Lok Sabha**
✅ Yes ✅ Yes **So,** **point**
**III** **is** **correct.**
**Chief** **Justice** **of** **India / SC** **Judge**
❌ No ✅ Yes **So,** **point**
**IV** **is** **correct.**
**So, only two of the above are part of the selection committees responsible for recommending** **appointments to the Chairperson and members of the National Human Rights Commission** **(NHRC) and the Lokpal** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The President addresses the Parliament only on the commencement of every Budget Session.
II. 	 The Presidential address is discussed on a Motion of Thanks, which is answered by the Prime Minister.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 87 of the Constitution** of India mandates a special address by the President of India to both Houses of Parliament. The President's speech outlines the government's policies and agenda for the year. This address is a joint address with members of both the Houses in attendance in the common hall.
● Article 87 provides for two special occasions on which the President addresses a joint sitting – First is to **address the new legislature after a General Election, second is to address the first sitting** **of the Parliament every year.** **So, statement I is not correct.**
○ The first session of the Parliament is generally the budget session, by convention. But there is no such requirement for the President to address the Parliament in the budget session per se. If the budget session takes place at any later stage in the year, the President is not obligated to give any address.
● Under **rule 15 of the Rules of Procedure**, discussion on the matters referred to in the President's address takes place on a Motion of Thanks moved by a member and seconded by another member. The Motion of Thanks is moved in each House by ruling party MPs, followed by discussions that last up to three or four days and **conclude with the Prime Minister replying to the points raised during** **the discussion**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● After the PM's reply, MPs vote on the motion of thanks and some may move amendments to the address. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● In India there is no set format for the President’s speech. The Constitution only states that the President shall inform Parliament of the cause of the summons.
● Under the US Constitution there is a predefined format according to which the President shall from time to time give the Congress information on the state of the Union, and recommend to their consideration such measures, which in his opinion are necessary and expedient.
● In India, the speech that the President reads is the viewpoint of the Government and written by it.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - State Human Rights Commission
II. 	 State Information Commission
III. 	 State Public Service Commission
IV. 	 State Election Commission For how many of the above bodies, chairperson/ members are appointed by the Governor, but can only be removed by the President?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **State Human Rights Commission (SHRC):** It is a statutory body established under the **Protection** **of Human Rights Act, 1993.** The **Governor appoints the Chairperson and members of the** **SHRC**. However, the **President of India has the authority to remove** the Chairperson and members, as per **Section 23 of the Act.** This removal can occur due to the proved misbehaviour, incapacity, or other specified reasons outlined in the Act. **So, point I is correct.**
● **State Information Commission (SIC):** It is a statutory body established under the Right to Information Act, 2005. **The Governor appoints the Chief Information Commissioner and** **other Information Commissioners of the State. The** **Governor has the power to remove** **these commissioners**, as per Section 17(1) and 17(3) of the Act. The Governor can remove a commissioner for misbehaviour or incapacity, or if they are found to be insolvent, convicted of an offense or engaging in other paid employment that conflicts with their duties. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **State Public Service Commission (SPSC):** It is a constitutional body and has its members **appointed by the Governor according to Article 316.** **However, the President of India has** **the authority** **to remove them,** but only after the Supreme Court has conducted an inquiry into allegations of misbehaviour, as outlined in Article 317. **So, point III is correct.**
● **State Election Commission (SEC):** It is a constitutional body headed by a State Election Commissioner who is appointed by the Governor of the state. As per Article 243K the Commissioner can only be removed except in like manner and on the like ground as a Judge of a High Court, hence the State Election Commissioner **cannot be removed by the Governor, but by the President.** **So,** **point IV is correct.**
**So, for only three of the above bodies, members are appointed by the Governor, but can only be** **removed by the President.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The State Government is empowered to
dissolve an Urban Local Body before the
expiry of its five-year term.
II. 	 The Urban Local Body reconstituted after
premature dissolution enjoys the full term of
five years.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **74****th** **Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992** provided for the creation of Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) by the State Governments within the purview of **Part IXA** of the Constitution. The State legislatures are empowered to pass relevant legislations under the sphere of Part IX to create, empower and devolve powers to the ULBs.
● As per **Article 243U of the Constitution of India**, an Urban Local Body (ULB) may be dissolved by the State Government before the expiry of its five-year term in accordance with state law. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **A municipality constituted upon the dissolution of a municipality before the expiration of** **its duration shall continue only for the remainder of the period** for which the dissolved municipality would have continued had it not been so dissolved. In other words, a municipality reconstituted after premature dissolution does not enjoy the full period of five years but remains in office only for the remainder of the period. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The Parliament can make laws on any matters
listed in List II of the Seventh Schedule for
the Union Territories (UTs).
II. 	 In case of any inconsistency between the
laws made by Parliament and those by the
UT legislature, the latter prevails.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Union Territories (UTs)** are the areas which are under the direct control and administration of the Central Government. For UTs with legislature as in Delhi, Puducherry and Jammu and Kashmir, some subjects from the State List have been delegated to the respective legislatures to legislate.
● It must be noted here that, **the legislative power of Parliament for the Union Territories on** **subjects of the State List remain unaffected even after establishing a local legislature for** **them,** hence the Parliament can make laws on any matters listed in List II of the Seventh Schedule for the Union Territories (UTs)**.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● When both the Union Territory legislature and Parliament legislate on the same subject, Parliament's law prevails. **In case of any inconsistency between the laws made by Parliament and those by** **the UT legislature, the latter becomes void to the extent of repugnancy,** unless the Parliamentary law allows the UT law to stand. This ensures central supremacy over UT legislation. **So,** **statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
The functioning of which one of the following
committees of the Parliament is quasi-judicial in
nature?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **quasi-judicial function** refers to the discharge of functions by any commission established by law, administrative officers, or bodies to determine the rights of those who appear before it. The body possesses the right to hold hearings on and conduct investigations into appeals and make decisions in the general manner of courts.
● Amongst the standing Parliamentary Committees, the **Committee on Privileges** and the **Ethics** **Committee** are said to have quasi-judicial powers.
● **The Committee on Privileges** deals with cases of breach of parliamentary privileges, which are the special rights and immunities enjoyed by both Houses and their members. It  **comprises 15 members** **in the Lok Sabha, and 10 members in the Rajya Sabha**. All members are nominated by the Speaker or Chairman.
● As for its quasi-judicial functioning, **the committee is vested with powers of:** o **Conducting inquiries** into alleged breaches of parliamentary privilege when referred by the
Speaker/Chairman or the House. o **Examining facts, hearing explanations**, and determining whether a breach has occurred. o **Summoning and ascribing oath:** The committee can summon any person to testify (with the
permission of the Presiding Officer) to testify and make him/her ascribe to an oath. o **Submitting recommendations** to the presiding officer or House—these are not legally
binding but are authoritative within parliamentary practice.
● Though it does not follow strict judicial procedures, its **investigative and adjudicatory role** ensures **discipline and accountability** within the Legislature. The committee also **handles petitions** **regarding disqualification of members** on grounds such as defection (if referred by the Speaker or Chairman). It submits reports either directly to the presiding officer or, in some cases, to the House, depending on the reference. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT):
I. - It is a constitutional body established under Article 323A of the Constitution of India.
II. 	 Its members have a fixed tenure of five years or hold office until the age of 60 years.
III. 	 Any appeal against the orders of the CAT lies with the High Court.
Which of the statements given above are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT)** is a specialized **quasi-judicial body** established under Article 323A of Constitution of India to adjudicate disputes related to recruitment and service conditions of public servants. It has jurisdiction over service matters of employees of the Union government and other authorities under central government control.
● The foundation of the formation of the CAT was laid down by the **42****nd** **Constitutional Amendment** **Act, 1976 which added Part XIVA to the Constitution** providing for Constitutional recognition and space for the Central Legislature to form a tribunal for disposing matters related to the employees of Central and State Government.
● Accordingly, **Administrative Tribunals Act of 1985** was passed by the Parliament, under which the CAT was institutionalized. Hence, **CAT is a statutory body**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The tribunal comprises a chairman (a sitting or retired High Court Judge) and both Administrative and Judicial Members, chosen for their expertise in relevant areas. **The members have a 5 years tenure with a** **maximum age to serve being 65 years** (68 for the Chairperson). **So, statement II is not correct**.
● **The appeals against the order of the CAT are placed before the High Courts.** Earlier the provisions of Article 323A provided that the appeals from tribunals established under the purview of it lie before the Supreme Court only, but the apex court in **L. Chandra Kumar versus Union of India** **and Ors, 1997** stated that the primary appellate body for orders from CAT should be the respective High Courts. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Governor of a State:
I. - He can summon a session of the Legislature for the purpose of a floor test.
II. 	 The powers to summon the State Legislature is solely vested with the Governor of the State.
III. 	 He cannot ask for a floor test if the legislature is already in session.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 174 of the Constitution of India** gives the Governor the power to summon from time to time ‘the House or each House of the Legislature of the State to meet at such time and place as he thinks fit..’
● The phrase **‘as he thinks fit’ is read as per Article 163 of the Constitution which says the** **Governor acts on the aid and advice of the Cabinet**. It essentially limits any discretionary power of the Governor only to cases where the Constitution expressly specifies that the Governor must act on his own and apply an independent mind.
● In the case of **Nabam Rebia and Bamang Felix versus Deputy Speaker, 2016** clarified that ‘In ordinary circumstances during the period when the Chief Minister and his Council of Ministers enjoys the confidence of the majority the House, the power vested with the Governor under Article 174 to summon, prorogue or dissolve the House is to be exercised as per the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers’.
● The Court further noted that ‘**in situations where the Government in power in opinion of the** **Governor has lost the confidence of the House, then it would be open to the Governor to** **exercise the powers vested with him under Article 174 on his own, and without any aid and** **advice’. So, statement I is correct.**
● In the same case, the Court expressly said that the powers to summon the House is not solely vested with the Governor. in 2016, a Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court in Nabam Rebia and Bamang Felix vs Deputy Speaker — the Arunachal Pradesh Assembly case — expressly said that the power to summon the House is not solely vested in the Governor. “When the House is in session, it is the Speaker who can call for a floor test. But when the Assembly is not in session, the Governor’s residuary powers under Article 163 allow him to call for a floor test. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● In the 2020 judgment (**Shivraj Singh Chouhan & Others v. Speaker**), the **Supreme Court** noted that when the Assembly is in session, the power to initiate a floor test lies with the Speaker. Conversely, when the **House is not in session, the Governor may, using his discretionary powers under** **Article 163,** summon the House and call for a floor test to ascertain the government’s majority
● However, **the Governor can still recommend a floor test if there is credible evidence** (e.g., letters from MLAs withdrawing support) that the ruling government may have lost its majority. In such cases, the Governor may direct the Chief Minister to prove majority, but the **actual conduct of the** **floor test lies within the Speaker’s domain,** as he controls the proceedings of the House. Hence, the **Governor can still recommend** a floor test when the Assembly is in session. **So, statement III** **is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
questions.questionText:
In which of the following cases is it mandatory for the Governor to reserve a bill for the assent of the President?
I. - If a bill is violative of any of the Directive Principles of State Policy
II.	 If a bill is in contravention to any of the laws passed by the Parliament
III.	 If a bill endangers the position of the High Court
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under **Article 200 of the Constitution of India**, when a Bill passed by the State Legislature is presented, the Governor **may assent to the Bill, withhold assent, reserve it for the President’s** **consideration, or return it (except a Money Bill) for reconsideration.**
● If the Bill, in the Governor’s opinion, undermines the constitutional position of the High Court, it **must** be reserved for the President under Article 200. **So, point III is correct.**
● Similarly, bills dealing with the **imposition of taxes on water or electricity** in certain inter-state contexts, as per Article 288(2), must be reserved. Additionally, during a **Financial Emergency** under Article 360(4)(a)(ii), any bill that falls within the scope of the emergency provisions requires Presidential scrutiny before becoming law.
● There are also bills that the Governor **may** reserve for the President’s assent for **specific** **constitutional objectives.** These include:
o Legislation seeking immunity from Articles 14 and 19—for example, laws related to the
acquisition of estates under Article 31A or those intended to implement Directive Principles under Article 31C. There is **no constitutional requirement to** **mandatorily reserve a bill** **that is violative of any of the Directive Principles of State Policy. So, point I is not** **correct.**
o Bills on **Concurrent List subjects**, if inconsistent with Union laws, may be reserved under
Article 254(2) to ensure their validity. **The bill in contravention to any of the laws passed** **by the Parliament is not mandatory to get reserved for Presidential assent**. **So, point** **II is not correct.**
● Further, Bills that may **not specifically fall under any of the above categories, yet may be** **reserved by the Governor for President's consideration** under Article 200.
● When a Governor reserves a Bill for the consideration of the President, the **President may either** **give assent or withhold assent to the Bill.**
● If the Bill is not a Money Bill, the President may direct the Governor to return it to the State Legislature with suggestions. The Legislature must reconsider it within six months, and if passed again, it must be resent to the President. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - A person who is not a member of the State Legislature cannot be appointed as a Minister in the State Cabinet.
II. 	 Ministers are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister, and they don’t have a security of tenure.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Articles 163 and 164 deal with the provisions of the Council of Ministers in the state.** Article 163 deals with the status of the Council of Ministers while Article 164 deals with the appointment, tenure, responsibility, qualifications, oath and salaries and allowances.
● The Chief Minister is appointed by the Governor and the other Ministers are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister, and they shall hold **office during the pleasure of the Governor**, that is they don’t have a security of tenure and can be removed at will of the Governor. **The Council** **of Ministers is collectively responsible** to the **State Legislative Assembly**. **So, Statement II is** **correct.**
● **Article 164(4) of the Constitution of India** allows for the appointment of a non-member of the State Legislature as a minister. However, this appointment is conditional. The individual needs **to get** **elected/nominated to the State Legislature (either the Legislative Assembly or Legislative** **Council) within six months** of their appointment. If they fail to do so, they cease to be a minister. **So, Statement I is not correct.**
o This provision ensures that while non-members can be considered for ministerial positions,
ultimately, ministers are expected to be accountable to the Legislature. The **same rule applies** **at the Centre under Article 75(5)** for Union Ministers. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Legislative Council:
I. - The strength of the Legislative Council of the State is determined by the State Legislative Assembly.
II. 	 The total number of members in the Legislative Council shall not exceed onethird of the total number of members in the Legislative Assembly of that State.
III. 	 One-third members of the Legislative Council are elected by members of local bodies in the State.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Legislative Council is the Upper House** (Vidhan Parishad) in the bicameral Legislature of some Indian states. It is a permanent body, similar to the Rajya Sabha at the Centre. Its creation or abolition is governed by constitutional and parliamentary provisions.
● The scheme of composition of a Legislative Council as laid down in the Constitution is tentative and not final. The **Article 172 itself authorizes the Parliament to modify or replace the same.** However, it has not enacted any such law so far. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● As per **Article 171,** the total number of members in the Legislative Council of a State **shall not exceed** **one-third of the total number of members in the Legislative Assembly** of that State and shall in no case be less than forty. However, the exact number of seats in a State’s Legislative Council is to be determined by Parliament by law**. So, Statement II is correct.**
● **Article 172** deals with the composition of the Legislative Council. In the Legislative Council:
o One-third members are to be elected by members of local bodies in the State. **So, Statement**
**III is correct.**
o One-twelfth to be elected by graduates of three years standing and residing
o One-twelfth by teachers in the State. Those teachers who have been engaged in teaching in
educational institutions (not lower than secondary school) within the state for at least three years.
o One-third by the members of the Legislative Assembly of the State.
o Remainder are to be nominated by the Governor of the State.
● Parliament can act and pass a law to abolish or create the Legislative Council. **As per Article 169,** Parliament may by law provide for the abolition or creation of the Legislative Council in **a State if the** **Legislative Assembly of that State passes a resolution** to that effect by a special majority (a majority of the total membership of the Assembly and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting). **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Governor of the State
II. 	 Advocate General of the State
III. 	 Speaker of the Assembly
IV. 	 Committees of the House The privileges of the State Legislature extend to
how many of the above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Article 194 of the Constitution of India provides for certain Privileges to the State Legislature. These privileges are certain special rights, immunities, and exemptions enjoyed by each House collectively and by its members individually. These are essential for the Legislature to discharge its constitutional functions effectively and are not available to ordinary citizens or bodies.
● Under **Article 194**, these privileges apply to: o Members of the State Legislature, o **Legislative Committees. So, point IV is correct.** o And those participating in legislative business, such as the **Advocate General**. **So, point II is**
**correct.**
● The **Governor**, though an integral part of the Legislature, **does not enjoy these privileges**, similar to how the **President** is excluded at the Union level. The office of the Governor has its own privileges separate from that of the State Legislature. **So, point I is not correct.**
● The **Speaker is a member** of the Legislative Assembly and the **presiding officer** of the House. **The** **individual privileges granted to members (like freedom of speech, freedom from civil** **arrest, etc.) also apply to the Speaker.** The collective privileges of the House (such as regulating its own procedures, publishing debates, and holding secret sessions) are often exercised through the Speaker as the head of the House. **So, point III is correct.** **So, the privileges of the State Legislature extend to only three of the above given** **functionaries/offices.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the State Legislature:
I. - Unlike Parliament, the Constitution of India does not provide for a joint sitting of the State Legislature to resolve deadlocks between the two Houses.
II. 	 If the State Assembly passes a bill a second time and it is rejected by the Legislative Council, it is deemed to be passed in the form originally passed by the Assembly.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constitution of India does not provide for a joint sitting of the State Legislature** (unlike Parliament) to resolve deadlocks between the two Houses. If a bill is passed by the Legislative Assembly and then rejected by the Legislative Council, the bill does not proceed to a joint sitting. Instead, the Legislative Assembly has the power to override the Council. **So, statement I is correct.**
● If the Assembly **passes the bill again** and sends it to the Council, and the Council either rejects it again, suggests unacceptable amendments, or fails to act within one month, then, the bill is **deemed to** **have been passed by both Houses** in the form originally passed by the Assembly the second time. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Special provisions related to Money Bill in the state Legislature includes:**
● Money bill is a government bill, introduced only by a minister.
● After passing the Legislative Assembly, it goes to the Legislative Council, which can only recommend changes within 14 days. The Assembly may accept or reject these suggestions. If accepted, the bill is passed in the amended form.
● If rejected or if the Council doesn t respond in 14 days, it is deemed passed in the original form.
● The Governor can give assent, withhold assent, or reserve the bill for the President but cannot return it to the Assembly.
● Similarly, if reserved, the President can give or withhold assent, but cannot return it for reconsideration.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Establishment of a three-tier institution of Panchayats
II. 	 Direct election to the Chairperson of the Gram Panchayat
III. 	 Organisation of State Finance Commission after every five years
IV. 	 Reservation for the Other Backward Classes in the Panchayats
How many of the above provisions under the Part IX of the Constitution are mandatory for the State Governments?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992**, gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions. It added **Part IX** and the **Eleventh Schedule** to the Constitution of India, enlisting various provisions related to Panchayati Raj Institution.
● The Part IX contains **both mandatory (compulsory) and voluntary provisions for Panchayati** **Raj Institutions (PRIs)** in India. Mandatory provisions are those that states are required to implement, while voluntary provisions allow for flexibility and adaptation based on state-specific needs.
● Mandatory provisions include: o **Gram Sabha**: Constitution of a Gram Sabha in every village or group of villages. o **Three-tier Panchayati Raj Structure**: Establishment of Panchayats at the village,
**intermediate**, and **district** levels in all states (except those with populations below 20 lakhs). **So, point I is not correct.** o **Direct Elections**: Members of all three levels of Panchayat are to be elected directly. o **Indirect Election of Chairpersons**: Chairpersons of Panchayats at the intermediate and
district levels to be elected indirectly by the elected members.
▪ While the chairperson of the intermediate and district levels must be elected indirectly, it is up to the **State Legislature to decide whether to conduct elections for the** **Chairperson of Gram Panchayat directly or indirectly. So, point II is not** **correct.** o **Minimum Age for Contesting Elections**: A person must be at least 21 years old to contest
Panchayat elections. o **Reservation of Seats**: For Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs) in proportion
to their population. One-third reservation for women, including in SC/ST categories and chairperson positions. **So, point IV is not correct.** o **Tenure of Panchayats**: Fixed tenure of five years for every Panchayat. o **Elections After Dissolution**: If dissolved prematurely, elections must be held within **six**
**months**. o **State Election Commission**: Establishment of a State Election Commission to supervise,
direct, and control Panchayat elections. o **State Finance Commission**: Constitution of a State Finance Commission every five years to
review and recommend financial distribution to Panchayats. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Voluntary Provisions of the 73rd Amendment Act include:**
o **Representation of MLAs/MPs**: Allowing elected members of the Parliament and State
Legislatures to be associated with Panchayats at different levels.
o **Reservation for Backward Classes**: Providing reservation of seats for Other Backward
Classes (OBCs). **So, point IV is not correct.**
o **Devolution of Powers and Responsibilities**: Granting Panchayats the necessary powers and
authority to function as institutions of self-government.
o **Assignment of Subjects**: Devolving responsibilities relating to the 29 subjects listed in the
Eleventh Schedule.
o **Financial Powers**: Granting financial autonomy to Panchayats through taxation, fees, and grants. **So, only one of the above provisions under the Part IX of the Constitution are mandatory for the** **State Governments.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Supreme Court’s Triple Test Formula:** The Supreme Court in its past judgements, laid out a triple test to be followed before provisioning reservation to Socially & Educationally Backward Classes (SEBCs)/Other Backward Classes (OBCs) in local bodies. The three parameters are:
● Set up a commission to conduct rigorous empirical inquiry into nature and implications of the backwardness quota in local bodies.
● Specify the proportion of reservation required to be provisioned local body wise in light of recommendation of the commission.
● In no case, such reservation should exceed 50% of the total seats reserved in favour of SCs/STs/OBCs taken together.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following;
I. - Conduct of elections to municipality and panchayats
II. 	 Preparation of electoral rolls
III. 	 Handling of election disputes
Which of the above is/are the functions of the State Election Commission (SEC)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **State Election Commission** is an independent, autonomous, Constitutional body created under Article 243K of the Constitution of India. It is responsible for ensuring free, fair, and unbiased elections to local bodies in the state, such as Panchayats and Municipalities. **So, point I is correct.**
● **The power of superintendence, direction, and control** over the preparation of electoral rolls and the conduct of elections to the Panchayats lies with the State Election Commission, which comprises a **State Election Commissioner** appointed by the **Governor**. **So, point II is correct.**
● **According to Article 243K (2)** of the Constitution of India, the Governor of a state determines the conditions of service and tenure of office for the State Election Commissioner subjected to any laws made by the State Legislature.
● The State Election Commissioner **can only be removed in the same manner and on the same** **grounds as a High Court Judge,** that is it can only be removed by the President post the motion of its removal is passed by two-thirds majority in the Parliament. The State Governor or the Legislature has no role to play in the removal of a State Election Commissioner.
● The role of State Election Commissioner and the State Election Commission is restricted to delimitation, conduct and declaration of the result. **The disputes to the elections are to be put forth before a** **body as is specified by the State Legislature via legislation.** **So, point III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Parliament, consider the following statements:
I. - The authoritative text of the bills that are presented in the Parliament shall be in English.
II. 	 The language for the transaction of business in the Parliament shall be either Hindi or English.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 348** of the Constitution of India says that the authoritative texts of all Bills to be introduced in either House of Parliament **shall be in the English language.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● Article 120 of the Constitution says that **business in Parliament shall be transacted in Hindi or** **in English.** The use of English was to be stopped after fifteen years from the commencement of this Constitution. But, the Official Languages Act, 1963 provided that the English language will continue to be used in addition to Hindi for all the official purposes of the Union and for the transaction of business in Parliament. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ Also, the Presiding Officer may permit any member to address the House in his mother-tongue.” **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Several Members of Parliament, especially from South India, are raising objections to Hindi or Sanskrit titles of some recent Bills. They are raising a question that there is a “constitutional requirement” for the Bills that have been written in English to have English titles. Article 348 (1B) states that the “authoritative texts, of all Bills to be introduced or amendments thereto be moved in either House of Parliament shall be in the English language.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Law Commission of India:
I. - It is a statutory body constituted by the Ministry of Law and Justice.
II. 	 It is always chaired by a retired Chief Justice of India.
III. 	 It is constituted after every five years.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Law Commission of India is a non-statutory body** and is constituted by a notification of the Ministry of Law and Justice, Government of India, with a definite terms of reference to carry out research in the field of law and the Commission makes recommendations to the Government (in the form of Reports) as per its terms of reference. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● It is established through a gazette notification to help the government review functioning of laws, suggest repealing of obsolete legislation, and make recommendations on matters referred to by the government.
● The commission is usually chaired by a **retired judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court,** and has legal scholars as members. Serving judges can also be appointed to the commission, according to the notification on the appointment of the new panel. **It is wholly an advisory body**; thus its recommendations are not binding on the Government of India. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● There is **no fixed tenure of the Law commission**, these are constituted as per the requirement and as terms of reference according to the gazette notification. **So, statement III is not correct.** **So, none of the above given statements is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - All the contracts of the Government of India are constitutionally required to be carried out in the name of the President of India.
II. 	 The Government contracts in the name of the President enjoys immunity from the provisions of any other statute passed by the Central or State Legislature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 294 to Article 300** of the Constitution of India provides for various rights and liabilities of the Government of India, in terms of Governmental contracts and, trades etc.
● As per **Article 299** of the Constitution of India, **all the contracts which are made under the** **Executive power of the Union or the State should be made in the name of the President or** **the respective Governor of the State**. If a contract is not made under the President’s or Governor’s name, such a contract will not be considered as a Government contract. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Under Clause 2 of the Article, it is made clear that the President or the Governor cannot be held personally liable for the non-performance of the contract.
● It must be noted here that, **the immunities granted to these two functionaries for not being** **liable to be sued in a Court of Law doesn’t extend to these contracts**, that is a contract entered into in the name of the President of India, cannot and will not create an immunity against the application of any statutory prescription imposing conditions on parties to an agreement, when the Government chooses to enter into a contract.
○ In other words, **the Government can sue as well as be sued in case of non-compliance** of the contract, as was made clear in the **Glock-Asia Pacific Limited versus the Union of India** **case**, 2023. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following with regard to National
Integration Council:
I. - It was set up in 1961 for combating
communalism, casteism and regionalism.
II. 	 It comprises the Prime Minister and Chief
Ministers of all States and Union Territories.
III. 	 It is mandated to meet at least twice a year.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **National Integration Council (NIC)** was set up in pursuance of the National Integration Conference in 1961 for combating communalism, casteism, regionalism etc. The **NIC is a standing** **body** to review all matters pertaining to National Integration and to make recommendations thereon. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The NIC **comprised of Prime Minister as Chairperson and Union Ministers, Leaders of** **Opposition in Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, Chief Ministers of all States and UTs with** **Legislatures**, Leaders of National Political Parties, Chairpersons of National Commissions, Media Persons, Representatives of Business, Representatives of Labour, Eminent Public Figure and Women’s Representatives as members. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Meetings of NIC are convened as per requirement from time to time**. There is no regular or specified time interval for convening meetings of the NIC. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the District Planning Committee (DPC):
I. - Its primary function is to consolidate
development plans prepared by Panchayats
and Municipalities in the district.
II. 	 All DPC members are elected from among
the elected members of Panchayats and
Municipalities.
III. 	 The District Commissioner acts as the ex-
officio chairperson of the DPC.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **District Planning Committee** (DPC) is a body established at the **district level** in every state in India, as per **Article 243ZD** of the Constitution. Its primary function is to consolidate development plans prepared by Panchayats (rural local governments) and Municipalities (urban local governments) within the district, and to **formulate a comprehensive district development plan.** **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Its composition is determined by the State Legislature through law, but the **Constitution provides** **certain minimum criteria**. Article 243ZD mandates that **at least four-fifths (80%)** of DPC members must be **elected from among the elected members of Panchayats and** **Municipalities**, in proportion to the rural and urban population. The election of the DPC is **conducted** **as per the procedure laid down by the State Legislature**, generally overseen by the **State** **Election Commission**. Only elected members of Panchayats and Municipalities participate in the election of DPC members. Nominated members and officials do not participate. **So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● The **Chairperson of the DPC** is chosen as per the provisions of the State Law. **So, statement III is** **not correct.**
● The State Legislature can make a law to decide how District Planning Committees will be formed, how their members will be selected, what planning functions they will handle, and how their chairpersons will be chosen. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Difference between DPC and MPC:**
● It is provided for under **Article 243ZE** and is constituted only in metropolitan areas; urban regions with a population exceeding 10lakh.
● Its role is to **prepare a draft development plan for the entire metropolitan** **region**, addressing issues such as urban infrastructure, land use planning, housing, transportation, and environmental management.
● At least two-thirds of the members of an MPC must be elected by, and from among, the elected members of Municipalities and other local bodies in the metropolitan area.
● One major difference between DPC and MPC is that while for the former all the elected members of Panchayats vote, while for the latter it is only the chairpersons of Panchayats that vote.
● While both DPC and MPC aim to promote decentralised and participatory planning, the DPC serves **a broader rural-urban integration role** at the district level, whereas the MPC caters specifically to the complex urban planning needs of rapidly growing metropolitan regions.
● Their existence reflects the constitutional commitment to multi-level planning in India’s federal and democratic governance structure.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following committees
recommended for the Panchayati Raj Institutions
(PRIs) to have compulsory powers of taxation?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Ashok Mehta Committee** was appointed by the Central Government in 1977 to examine the weaknesses in the functioning of the Panchayati Raj system and suggest measures for its revitalisation. The committee submitted its report in August 1978.
● One of the major themes it discussed was the **financial autonomy of the Panchayats** in this line it recommended for **devolution of taxation powers to Panchayati Raj Institutions.**
o In the 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1993, **the provision of taxation was given to**
**Panchayats, but it is a voluntary provision.** It is up to the States to grant power to Panchayats for taxation or not.
● Some of the key recommendations of the committee were:
o **Two-Tier System:** The traditional three-tier structure was to be replaced with a two-tier
system and establish Zila Parishad at the district level as the main executive body with Mandal Panchayat below it, comprising a group of villages with a population of 15,000–20,000.
o **Empowerment of Zila Parishad:** The Zila Parishad was to function as the executive body,
responsible for district-level planning, development, and administration.
o **Financial Autonomy:** PRIs should be granted compulsory powers of taxation to mobilise their
own financial resources, reducing overdependence on state governments.
o **Social Justice and Oversight:** A system of regular social audits by district-level agencies and
legislative committees to ensure proper utilisation of funds, especially for vulnerable groups.
o **Reservation of seats** for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in Panchayati Raj bodies in
proportion to their population.
o  **Independent Judiciary at Grassroots:** Nyaya Panchayats were to be constituted as separate
entities from development panchayats and should be presided over by qualified judges, ensuring impartial justice.
o  **Election Reforms:** Panchayat elections to be conducted by the Chief Electoral Officer of the
state in consultation with the Chief Election Commissioner. In case of supersession of PRIs, elections must be held within six months to restore democratic functioning.
o **Administrative and Ministerial Support:** A Minister for Panchayati Raj should be appointed
in the State Council of Ministers to oversee PRI affairs. Emphasis was placed on involving voluntary agencies to generate public awareness and participation.
o **Constitutional Backing:** Recommended that Panchayati Raj Institutions be accorded
constitutional status, to provide continuity, legitimacy, and protection from arbitrary state interference. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
• **BALWANT RAI MEHTA COMMITTEE, 1957** **(Committee on community development programme)**
• Recommended for the Scheme of Democratic decentralisation. • Three tier system of Panchayati Raj Institutions - Village Panchayat (directly elected), Panchayat Samiti and Zila Parishad (indirectly elected, executive bodies), • **ASHOK MEHTA COMMITTEE, 1977** **(Committee on Panchayati Raj Institutions)**
• Two tier system with Zila Parishad as the executive authority and Mandal Panchayat below it. • The PRIs to have compulsory powers of taxation that is they should have their own financial resources. • Nyay Panchayats should be established as a separate body from the Development Panchayats • Establishment of the office of Minister of Panchayati Raj in the State Council of Ministers. • Reservation of seats for SCs & STs. • Constitutional recognition to PRIs • **GVK RAO COMMITTEE, 1985** **(Committee to review the existing arrangement for rural development** **and poverty alleviation)**
• Called PRIs as grass without roots • The PRIs at the district and lower levels should be designed as important role with respect to planning, implementation and monitoring of rural development programme. • Creation of the post of District Development Commissioner • **LM SINGHVI COMMITTEE, 1986** **(Committee on Revitalization of PRIs for democracy and development)**
• Called Gram Sabha as EMBODIMENT OF DIRECT DEMOCRACY • Constitutional status for PRIs • Constitutional provisions for free and fair elections for PRIs • Nyaya Panchayats to be established for a cluster of villages. • Establishment of judicial tribunals at State levels to adjudicate controversies about elections to PRIs • **THUNGON COMMITTEE, 1988** **(Committee on District Planning)**
• Constitutional recognition to PRIs • Three tier system of PRIs with Zila Parishad as pivot • A detailed list of subjects for PRIs to be prepared and incorporated in the Constitution. • Reservation in PRIs on the basis of population
• Reservation for women in Panchayats • Establishment of State Finance Commission • District Collector to be appointed as CEO of Zila Parishad • **GADGIL COMMITTEE, 1988** **(Committee to recommend How best PRIs can be made effective)**
• Constitutional recognition to PRIs • Three tier system of PRIs • Members of all three levels to be elected directly

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which of the following bills/resolutions will not
lapse on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha?
I. - A bill for which the President has notified a
joint sitting of both the Houses
II. 	 A bill passed by the Lok Sabha but pending
in the Rajya Sabha
III. 	 A bill returned by the President for the
reconsideration
IV. 	 A resolution for the removal of a High Court
judge pending in the Lok Sabha
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Dissolution** of the House means the end of the life of the Lok Sabha either by an order made by the President under Article 85 (2) (b) of the Constitution or on the expiration of the period of five years from the date appointed for its first meeting. Dissolution puts an end to the representative character of the individuals who at the time composed the Lok Sabha.
● On adjournment of the Lok Sabha or its adjournment sine die, the pending business does not lapse. However, all business pending before the House or any of its Committee lapse on dissolution.
● The position with respect to lapsing of bills on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha is as follows: ○ A bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses (whether originating in the Lok Sabha or transmitted to it by the Rajya Sabha). ○ **A bill not passed by the two Houses due to disagreement and if the president has** **notified the holding of a joint sitting before the dissolution of Lok Sabha, does not** **lapse.** **So, point I is correct.** ○ **A bill passed by the Lok Sabha but pending in the Rajya Sabha lapses. So, point II is** **not correct.** ○ A bill pending in the Rajya Sabha but not passed by the Lok Sabha does not lapse. ○ A bill passed by both Houses but pending assent of the president does not lapse. ○ **A bill passed by both Houses but returned by the president for reconsideration of** **Houses does not lapse. So, point III is correct.** ○ A removal motion for the removal of a judge does not lapse on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Scheduled Areas:
I. - Their boundaries are determined by the
Parliament.
II. 	 The Governor can decide whether or not a
law of the Parliament applies to the area.
III. 	 The decrees of the High Court apply to the
Scheduled Area like other parts of the State.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Fifth Schedule of the Constitution** **of India** deals with the administration and control of Scheduled areas and Scheduled tribes in any state except the four states of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram. For these areas the whole of the normal administrative machinery operating in a State is not extended to the Scheduled Areas and the Central Government has somewhat greater responsibilities for these areas.
● **The President** is empowered to declare any area to be a scheduled area. He can also **increase or** **decrease its area, alter its boundary lines**, rescind such designation or make fresh orders for such designation on an area in consultation with the Governor of the State concerned. **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
● The **Governor** of the respective State is empowered to **direct that any particular law enacted by** **Parliament or the State Legislature shall not apply** to a Scheduled Area or shall apply with specified modifications or exceptions. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Scheduled Areas are part of the respective states, and the **jurisdiction of the High Court extends** to all parts of the State, including Scheduled Areas. There is no constitutional bar on the applicability of High Court decrees to Scheduled Areas.  **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Fifth** **Schedule** **(Scheduled Areas & Scheduled Tribes)**
**Sixth** **Schedule** **(Tribal Areas)**
● Applicable to: **1)** Himachal Pradesh **2)** Rajasthan **3)** Gujrat **4)** Maharashtra **5)** Madhya Pradesh **6)** Jharkhand **7)** Odisha **8)** Chhattisgarh **9)** Andhra Pradesh **10)** Telangana
● Applicable to states mentioned in the Sixth Schedule only:
**1)** **Assam** **2)** **Meghalaya** **3)** **Mizoram** **4)** **Tripura**
● Declaration of Scheduled Areas and any increase or decrease in its area is reserved with the **President** (in consultation with the Governor concerned)
● The **Governor i**s empowered to organise or reorganise the autonomous district.
● **The executive power of a State** **extends to the scheduled areas** **therein**. But the Governor has a special responsibility regarding such areas. He/she has to submit a report to the President regarding the administration of such areas, annually or whenever so required by the president.
● The executive power of the Centre extends to giving directions to the states regarding the administration of such areas.
● Executive authority of the State concerned is applicable
● **Tribes Advisory Council (TAC):** 20 membered at the State level – three-fourth of its members are to be from the Scheduled Tribes
o TAC has to be formed not
only for the States in Fifth Schedule, but also in the States where there is sufficient number of tribals present.
● **District** **Council**: Each autonomous district has a 30 membered District Council, 26 of it are elected and 4 are nominated.
● This District Council enjoys executive, legislative and administrative powers.
● There is **no change in the** **jurisdiction of High Courts.**
● The **District** **Councils** **are** **empowered to convene local** **courts (council courts),** The jurisdiction of High Courts over these council courts are as decided by the Governor.
● The **Governor** is empowered to direct that any law of Parliament or State Legislature doesn’t apply to scheduled areas with specific modifications and exceptions.
● The laws passed by the Parliament or the State legislature do not apply to autonomous districts or apply with specific modifications or exceptions.
● The power of direction in this regard lies either with the President or the Governor:
o **Assam – Governor** o **Meghalaya, Mizoram &**
**Tripura – President**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs in the context of
Panchayati Raj Institutions:
Committee Recommendation
I - Balwant Rai Mehta Committee
Democratic decentralisation
II. - G.V. K. Rao Committee Constitutional recognition to Panchayati Raj
III. - L M Singhvi Committee District is the proper unit for planning and development
How many of the above pairs are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Balwant Rai Mehta Committee:** In January 1957, the Government of India appointed a committee to examine the working of the Community Development Programme (1952) and the National Extension Service (1953) and to suggest measures for their better working.
