The chairman of this committee was Balwant Rai G Mehta. The committee submitted its report in November 1957 and recommended the establishment of the scheme of **‘democratic decentralisation’, which ultimately came to be** **known as Panchayati Raj**. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **G.V.K. Rao Committee:** The Committee to review the existing Administrative Arrangements for Rural Development and Poverty Alleviation Programmes under the chairmanship of G.V.K. Rao was appointed by the Planning Commission in 1985. The Committee recommended that the district level body, that is, the **Zila Parishad** should be of pivotal importance in the scheme of democratic decentralisation. It stated that **“the district is the proper unit for planning and development** and the Zila Parishad should become the principal body for management of all development programmes
which can be handled at that level. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **L M Singhvi Committee:** In 1986, Rajiv Gandhi government appointed a committee to prepare a concept paper on ‘Revitalisation of Panchayati Raj Institutions for Democracy and Development’ under the chairmanship of L.M. Singhvi. The committee recommended that **the Panchayati Raj institutions** **should be constitutionally recognised,** protected and preserved.  **So, pair III is not correctly** **matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The Parliamentary Committees are
constituted by the President on the advice of
the Prime Minister.
II. 	 The chairperson of all the Parliamentary
committees is appointed by the respective
Presiding officer.
III. 	 The tenure of all Parliamentary Committees
is concurrent with that of the Lok Sabha.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Parliamentary Committees play a crucial role in **ensuring government accountability by reviewing** **its policies and programmes** and recommending improvements. They have the authority to summon ministers and officials for questioning on matters of policy and administration. These committees also examine bills in detail before they are passed, ensuring a thorough legislative review. Additionally, they can suggest amendments to align proposed laws with constitutional and legal standards.
● A Parliamentary Committee consists of Members of Parliament (MPs) who are either elected or appointed from among the members of the House or nominated by the **Presiding Officers** of the respective Houses. **The commissioning of a committee is an internal business of the** **Parliament**, and the President in its official capacity has no role to play, rather **it is the Speaker or** **the Chairman which appoints the members of the committees**. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● **The Chairperson of each of the Parliamentary Committees is appointed by the Chairman,** **Rajya Sabha and by the Speaker in Lok Sabha** from amongst the members of the Committee. These committees function under the guidance of the Presiding Officers of the respective Houses and submit their reports and recommendations to them. **So, Statement II is correct.**
● Parliamentary Committees in India are **broadly classified into two types**: Standing Committees and Ad hoc Committees. **Standing Committees are permanent in nature** and are constituted by Parliament to focus on specific areas of public policy or administration. In contrast, **Ad hoc** **Committees are temporary bodies** set up for a specific purpose and a limited time frame. The Standing Committees are continuous bodies that don't dissolve with the dissolution of the Lok Sabha, rather it is just their members who are changed. **Hence, neither the ad hoc or the standing** **committees have their tenure co-terminus with the Lok Sabha. So, statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements is **not**
correct with regard to the legislative powers of
the Governor?

options.a:
The Governor cannot give assent to a bill the
second time, if the same was reserved for the
President at the first instance.
options.b:
A money bill cannot be introduced in the State
Legislature without prior recommendation of
the Governor.
options.c:
It is a constitutional responsibility of the
Governor to address the State Legislature at
the first sitting of the year.
options.d:
The veto powers of the Governor are
concurrent with that of the President.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Chapter IV of the Part VI of the Constitution of India** provides for the legislative powers of the Governor in relation to the State Legislature. These powers include:
o Powers to summon, prorogue and dissolve the Legislative Assembly
o Sending messages to the Legislature.
o Nominating 1/6th members to the Legislative Council and 1 Anglo-Indian (if needed) to the
Assembly.
o Providing assent to the bills passed by the Legislature, withholding, returning (if not a money bill),
or reserving for the President.
o Promulgating ordinances when Legislature is not in session
● Article 111 of the Constitution of India, provides for certain vetoing powers to the **President** (it must be remembered that the term veto is not mentioned in the Constitution, it is an interpretative fact), accordingly **the President can assent, withhold, return the bill.**
● While in the case of the **Governor** he/she can either grant assent**, withhold or reserve the bill for** **the President.** At the maximum it can reserve the bill for the consideration of the President. Hence, the President has much broader vetoing powers than the Governor.
● As per **Article 201** of the Constitution, **when the Governor reserves a bill for the consideration** **of the President, he will not have any further role in the enactment of the bill**. If the bill is returned by the President for the reconsideration of the House or Houses and is passed again, the bill must be presented again for the Presidential assent only. If the President gives his assent to the bill, it becomes an Act. This meant that the assent of the Governor was no longer needed.
● Like Parliament, a **Money Bill cannot be introduced in a State Legislature** without the prior recommendation of the Governor as per Article 207(1).
● As per Article 176, the **Governor must address the State Legislature at the beginning** of the first session every year and after each general election, and it is the Constitutional responsibility of the office of the Governor. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to Public Accounts Committee (PAC):
I. - Its members are elected by both Houses
of Parliament through proportional
representation by means of the single
transferable vote.
II. 	 Ministers are prohibited from being members
of the PAC.
III. 	 Each member serves a term of one year, after
which the committee is reconstituted.
Select the correct answer from the codes given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Public Accounts Committee (PAC)** is one of the three standing financial committees of the Parliament. It examines the government's revenue and expenditure, ensuring public funds are spent efficiently and legally. **It reviews audit reports from the Comptroller and Auditor General** **(CAG)**, investigating financial irregularities and recommending corrective measures.
● It currently comprises **22 members, 15 from the Lok Sabha, and 7 from the Rajya Sabha**. The members are **elected annually** by both Houses of Parliament **through proportional** **representation by means of the single transferable vote**, ensuring equitable representation of political parties.  **So, Statement I is correct.**
o **Ministers are prohibited from being members of the PAC,** a rule designed to prevent
executive influence over legislative review. This safeguard helps maintain the committee’s independence and objectivity in examining government expenditure and financial administration. **So, Statement II is correct.**
● The **term of office** for PAC members is **one year**. Each member serves a term of one year, after which the **committee is reconstituted**. Despite the brief tenure, the PAC ensures continuity and institutional expertise through the reappointment of members and consistent support from the parliamentary secretariat and CAG audit reports. **So, Statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Not less than one-third of the total number of seats to be filled by direct election at all levels of Panchayats are reserved for women.
II. 	 Reservation for the Scheduled Castes in the Panchayats are in proportion to their population in the Panchayat area.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Article 243D of the Constitution of India deals with **reservations of seats and offices in Panchayati** **Raj Institutions** to ensure representation of historically marginalised groups, namely Scheduled Castes (SCs), Scheduled Tribes (STs), and women.
● Seats are to be reserved for **Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes** in every Panchayat (village, intermediate, and district levels), **in proportion to their population** in that Panchayat area. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● As per Part IX of the Constitution, **not less than one-third of the total number of seats at all** **levels should be reserved for women.** It must be noted that there is no maximum ceiling on the quantum of reservation to women in Panchayats. **So, statement I is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Reservation of Chairperson Offices**:
● The **offices of Chairpersons** at all levels of Panchayats shall also be reserved for **SCs,** **STs, and women**. The number of Chairperson posts reserved for SCs and STs should be in proportion to their population in the state.
● The **exact manner of reservation** (how many and which posts) is to be **decided** **by the State Legislature** through legislation.
● The number of Chairperson posts reserved for SCs and STs at each level should reflect their **proportion in the total state population**.
● **Not less than one-third** of all Chairperson posts at each level must be **reserved for** **women**, including SC/ST women.
● These reservations for Chairpersons should be rotated among different Panchayats at each level.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Panchayat Extension to Scheduled Areas (PESA) Act, 1996:
I. - The post of chairperson of the Panchayat is reserved for Scheduled Tribes.
II. 	 The ownership of the Minor Forest Produce lies with the Panchayats.
III.	 Recommendation of the Gram Sabha or Panchayat is mandatory before any land acquisition.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Panchayats (Extension to the Scheduled Areas) Act 1996** was enacted to extend the provisions of Part IX of the constitution to the **Fifth Schedule Areas** with certain modifications. It was aimed at providing **greater autonomy to the Scheduled areas,** which are predominantly tribal in nature, and protect their rights over land and forest.
● The Panchayati Raj Institutions established under the Act are provided with greater autonomy and special provisions to ensure the effectiveness of local Government in the region.
● Unlike Panchayats constituted elsewhere in India, for the Panchayats under PESA **no less than 50% of** **the total seats** in every Panchayat are to be reserved for Scheduled Tribes and **all Chairperson** **positions across all Panchayat levels are reserved for STs. So, Statement I is correct.**
● The Panchayats under PESA also exercise **ownership over minor forest produce,** enabling tribal economic empowerment.  **So, Statement II is correct.**
● Mandatory recommendation of **Gram Sabha or Panchayat** is needed for granting mining leases or concessions for minor minerals by auction and also **before land acquisition** or resettlement in Scheduled Areas. **So, Statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Panchayat Extension to Scheduled Areas (PESA) Act, 1996:**
● **Special Powers of Grama Sabha under PESA:** Holds significant powers in utilisation and management of natural resources in the Schedule areas.
o Grama Sabha is a **statutory body comprising all adult members** listed
in the village Panchayat electoral roll. o The Gram **Sabha identifies, approves, and oversees the planning and**
**implementation** of development projects. o Gram Sabha should be consulted before **any development projects or**
**activities** are undertaken in the Scheduled Areas. o Panchayats must obtain **Gram Sabha certification for fund utilisation**
in approved projects. o **Mandatory recommendation** of Gram Sabha or Panchayat is needed for
granting **mining leases or concessions** **for minor minerals** by auction and also **before land acquisition** or resettlement in Scheduled Areas.
● **Special Powers of Panchayats under PESA:** They are constitutionally empowered institutions of self-governance at village/intermediate/district level with the following functions.
o Plans and implements projects in consultation with and with approval from
the Gram Sabha. o Coordinate project planning and implementation at the state and local level. o Enforces **prohibition or regulates intoxicants** to preserve tribal health
and customs. o Exercises **ownership over minor forest produce,** enabling tribal
economic empowerment. o Can **prevent alienation of tribal land** and restore unlawfully transferred
lands. o Manages and **regulates village markets** to protect tribal economic
interests. o Supervises social sector institutions like schools and health centres at the
village level. o Controls **local development plans and tribal sub-plans** to ensure
targeted delivery. o Planning and management of **minor water bodies** in the Schedule Areas.
● **Other Significant Provisions:** o **Reservation in Panchayats:** In Scheduled Areas, **not less than 50% of**
**the total seats** in every Panchayat are to be reserved for Scheduled Tribes. All **Chairperson positions** across all Panchayat levels are reserved for STs**.** o **Nomination of STs in Higher Panchayats:** State governments can
nominate members of STs to intermediate or district-level Panchayats if they are otherwise unrepresented, but such nomination should not exceed onetenth of the total elected members. o **Overriding Effect on Inconsistent Laws:** Any law inconsistent with the
provisions of the PESA Act shall cease to be in force after one year from the date of its enactment. However, existing Panchayats may continue until the expiry of their term.
